Higher-order adaptive integration buys deeper deterministic minimization and a small regularization effect, but nothing a cheaper, well-tuned first-order baseline does not already provide.

AI.tldr: Higher-order Runge-Kutta Adam variant loses to plain Adam under compute-matched evaluation, but fixing implementation reveals fragile training benefits that don't translate to test accuracy improvements.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to explore whether higher-order Runge-Kutta (RK) integrators, inspired by interpreting optimizers as gradient-flow discretizations, can provide better optimization for neural networks compared to standard first-order methods like Adam.
AI.method: The authors build an Adam variant using Bogacki-Shampine 3(2) RK pair with FSAL reuse and local-error step control, then evaluate it under a strict compute-matched protocol where all methods get the same gradient-evaluation budget. They instrument the optimizer to analyze its adaptive behavior and fix implementation issues like the true reject branch and error on the applied map.
AI.result: Under compute-matched evaluation, the RK variant loses to plain Adam on training loss. The adaptivity is found to be illusory - error stays below tolerance, step size pins at growth cap, and trajectories remain bit-identical across different tolerances. When fixed, the optimizer achieves 40x lower training loss in full-batch training but gains are fragile to initial step size and don't improve test accuracy. Gradient averaging provides implicit regularization but is outperformed by cheaper first-order methods like RMSprop and NAdam.
AI.conclusion: Higher-order adaptive integration enables deeper deterministic minimization and small regularization effects, but these benefits don't justify the 3-4x computational cost compared to well-tuned first-order baselines that match or exceed its performance at much lower cost.

title: VLT: A Vision-Language-Time Series Multimodal Foundation Model for Industrial Intelligence
comment: 18 pages, 13 figures, and 13 tables, including supplementary material. Haiteng Wang and Jingheng Yan contributed equally to this work
summary: Industrial time series serve as the foundation for Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) to ensure the reliability and safety of industrial equipment such as aero-engines. However, existing approaches are typically limited to single-modality modeling, which restricts their generalization in complex scenarios. Although recent advances in large language models (LLMs) provide new opportunities for multimodal learning, bridging continuous time-series signals and discrete textual semantics remains an open challenge. To this end, we propose VLT, a multimodal foundation model that jointly models time-series, frequency-spectrum visual representations, and textual knowledge. A key insight is to utilize the frequency spectrum as a visual bridge to connect continuous temporal signals with discrete semantics. Specifically, a Time-aware Mixture-of-Experts (Time-MoE) is designed to capture heterogeneous temporal dynamics, while a Frequency-Text Augmented Learner enables joint modeling of spectral and semantic features within a shared representation space. Furthermore, a time-centric gradient alignment mechanism is introduced to mitigate cross-modal optimization conflicts via gradient normalization and reliability-aware dynamic reweighting. Extensive experiments on multiple industrial datasets demonstrate that VLT outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior robustness and generalization under few-shot, noisy, and incomplete-modality settings.

AI.tldr: VLT is a multimodal foundation model that bridges industrial time-series signals with textual semantics using frequency spectrum as a visual bridge, achieving superior performance in PHM tasks under challenging conditions.
AI.motivation: Existing industrial time-series approaches are limited to single-modality modeling, restricting generalization in complex scenarios. While LLMs offer multimodal learning opportunities, bridging continuous time-series signals with discrete textual semantics remains challenging.
AI.method: Proposes VLT model with: 1) Frequency spectrum as visual bridge between temporal signals and semantics, 2) Time-aware Mixture-of-Experts (Time-MoE) for heterogeneous temporal dynamics, 3) Frequency-Text Augmented Learner for joint spectral-semantic modeling, 4) Time-centric gradient alignment with gradient normalization and reliability-aware dynamic reweighting to mitigate cross-modal optimization conflicts.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple industrial datasets show VLT outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior robustness and generalization under few-shot, noisy, and incomplete-modality settings.
AI.conclusion: VLT successfully bridges the gap between continuous time-series signals and discrete textual semantics, providing an effective multimodal foundation model for industrial PHM with strong generalization capabilities across challenging scenarios.

title: A Continuous-Time Reinforcement Learning Framework for Fine-Tuning Discrete Diffusion Models
summary: We formulate reinforcement learning (RL) in continuous time with discrete state spaces and possibly arbitrary action spaces via a stochastic control approach, where the state dynamics are modeled as a controlled continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC). We consider policy optimization problems and derive the corresponding policy gradient methods, leading to continuous-time variants of proximal policy optimization (PPO) and group relative policy optimization (GRPO). As a primary application, we develop a complete continuous-time RL framework for fine-tuning score-based discrete diffusion models. The proposed framework enables reward-driven optimization without requiring differentiability on the reward signals. In contrast to the existing GRPO-based approaches that only rely on terminal rewards, our formulation allows intermediate reward or advantage signals to be incorporated throughout the denoising trajectory. Importantly, when specialized to masked diffusion models (MDMs), our framework encompasses a rich class of policy parameterizations over the vocabulary simplex with analytically tractable probability ratios, providing a unified perspective on exploration and policy optimization in MDMs. For masked diffusion large language models (dLLMs), we further propose trajectory subsampling techniques to efficiently estimate computationally prohibitive trajectory likelihoods, reducing the computational cost of computing per-position probability ratios. We showcase the effectiveness of our methods on both low-dimensional entropy-regularized optimization problems and RL post-training of dLLMs on mathematical reasoning and coding tasks.

AI.tldr: A continuous-time reinforcement learning framework for discrete state spaces using controlled Markov chains, applied to fine-tuning diffusion models with policy gradient methods like PPO and GRPO variants.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the need for RL in continuous-time settings with discrete state spaces, particularly for optimizing diffusion models. Current approaches have limitations: they require differentiable reward signals, often only use terminal rewards, and have computational challenges with trajectory likelihoods in large models.
AI.method: The authors formulate RL in continuous time using controlled continuous-time Markov chains (CTMCs). They derive policy gradient methods for policy optimization, creating continuous-time variants of PPO and GRPO. For diffusion models specifically, they develop a complete RL framework for fine-tuning score-based discrete diffusion models, with special techniques for masked diffusion models (including trajectory subsampling for large language models).
AI.result: The framework enables reward-driven optimization without requiring differentiable reward signals and allows incorporation of intermediate reward/advantage signals throughout trajectories (not just terminal rewards). For masked diffusion models, it provides analytically tractable probability ratios and unified exploration/policy optimization. The methods show effectiveness on low-dimensional entropy-regularized optimization problems and RL post-training of diffusion LLMs on mathematical reasoning and coding tasks.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a novel continuous-time RL framework that successfully addresses limitations in existing approaches, particularly for diffusion model optimization, with practical applications demonstrated on both simple problems and complex language model tasks.

title: RetroAgent: Harnessing LLMs to Search Over Structured Memory for Agentic Retrosynthesis Planning
summary: Multi-step retrosynthesis planning seeks to decompose a target molecule into commercially available building blocks through a sequence of feasible reactions. The vast combinatorial search space makes this task challenging even for expert chemists. Traditional methods combine tree search with offline-trained value networks that score candidates in isolation, without reasoning about complete multi-step routes. Recent work leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) for this task, but relies on simple interfaces that limit exploration of the full search space. We introduce RetroAgent, an LLM agent that bridges symbolic search and neural reasoning through a harness with structured memory. Through memory and chemistry tools, the agent observes the full search state, including explored routes, available alternatives, and properties of intermediates, enabling informed decisions grounded in both global progress and domain knowledge. Experiments on in-distribution and out-of-distribution benchmarks demonstrate that RetroAgent delivers strong performance and generalization.

AI.tldr: RetroAgent is an LLM-based agent that combines symbolic search with neural reasoning for multi-step retrosynthesis planning, outperforming traditional methods through structured memory and chemistry-aware tools.
AI.motivation: Multi-step retrosynthesis planning has a vast combinatorial search space that challenges even expert chemists. Traditional methods use offline-trained value networks that score candidates in isolation without reasoning about complete routes. Recent LLM approaches have simple interfaces that limit full search space exploration.
AI.method: RetroAgent is an LLM agent that bridges symbolic search and neural reasoning through a harness with structured memory. It uses memory and chemistry tools to observe full search state including explored routes, available alternatives, and intermediate properties, enabling informed decisions grounded in global progress and domain knowledge.
AI.result: Experiments on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution benchmarks demonstrate that RetroAgent delivers strong performance and generalization compared to traditional methods.
AI.conclusion: RetroAgent effectively combines symbolic search capabilities with neural reasoning through structured memory, providing a more informed and effective approach to multi-step retrosynthesis planning that generalizes well to different chemical domains.

title: xHC: Expanded Hyper-Connections
summary: Hyper-Connections (HC) expand the residual stream of Transformers into $N$ parallel streams, providing a form of memory scaling beyond model width and depth. Manifold-Constrained HC (mHC) stabilizes this formulation at scale. The large gains from $N{=}1$ to $N{=}4$ suggest residual-stream expansion as a promising scaling axis. However, existing HC-family methods typically stop at $N{=}4$. Our experiments reveal why: scaling mHC beyond this point yields diminishing performance gains and rapidly increasing training cost. We attribute this limitation to two bottlenecks: insufficient write-back information for an expanding number of streams and residual-mixing generation whose cost scales cubically with $N$. To address both bottlenecks, we propose xHC (Expanded Hyper-Connections), the first HC-family method to achieve meaningful expansion beyond $N{=}4$. xHC combines temporal feature augmentation for richer write-back with a sparse residual-stream architecture that updates only $k=4$ of the $N=16$ streams while retaining dense access to the full residual state. Across 18B and 28B MoE models, xHC delivers strong and consistent downstream improvements. On an 18B MoE model, xHC improves the average downstream score by 4.0 points over mHC, while adding only modest training FLOPs over the vanilla baseline. Scaling-law experiments show that the vanilla and mHC require $1.50\times$ and $1.19\times$ the compute of xHC, respectively, to reach the same loss. Practical large-$N$ training also requires controlling memory traffic from the expanded residual state. We therefore introduce xHC-Flash, which reduces the per-sublayer memory traffic from $73.5C$ to $40C$, comparable to the $34C$ required by mHC at $N{=}4$, while retaining the gains of full xHC. Together, xHC and xHC-Flash make large-$N$ residual-stream expansion effective and practical for LLM pre-training.

AI.tldr: Expanded Hyper-Connections (xHC) enables effective residual-stream expansion beyond N=4 streams by addressing bottlenecks in existing HC methods, achieving meaningful performance improvements with practical memory efficiency.
AI.motivation: Hyper-Connections expand Transformer residual streams into parallel streams for memory scaling, but existing methods stop at N=4 due to diminishing performance gains and rapidly increasing training costs at larger N.
AI.method: xHC addresses two bottlenecks: 1) temporal feature augmentation for richer write-back information, and 2) sparse residual-stream architecture that updates only k=4 of N=16 streams while maintaining dense access to full residual state. Also introduces xHC-Flash to reduce memory traffic.
AI.result: On 18B MoE models, xHC improves average downstream score by 4.0 points over mHC while adding modest training FLOPs. Scaling experiments show vanilla and mHC require 1.50× and 1.19× more compute than xHC to reach same loss. xHC-Flash reduces memory traffic from 73.5C to 40C while retaining gains.
AI.conclusion: xHC and xHC-Flash make large-N residual-stream expansion effective and practical for LLM pre-training, overcoming previous limitations and providing a viable scaling axis beyond traditional width/depth scaling.

title: WrAFT: a Modularized Automated Writing Evaluation System for Argumentative Essays
summary: This study presents WrAFT, a Writing Assessment and Feedback Tool, that delivers both accurate and reliable scores and effective comprehensive feedback to argumentative essays. WrAFT adopts a modular design by dividing automated writing evaluation (AWE) tasks into scoring, surface-level feedback, and deep-level feedback. In building the system, various Large Language Models (LLMs) have been evaluated, including LLaMA-3.3-70B-Instruct, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.7, through both direct prompting and supervised fine-tuning approaches. A proprietary dataset of 480 TOEFL Independent Writing essays with official benchmark scores was utilized. Benchmark-based evaluation shows that WrAFT achieves state-of-the-art performance in scoring, with a quadratic weighted kappa (QWK) of 0.84 and a root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.44 against official scores on a scale of 0-5. Human evaluation of system-generated feedback also reveals high approval ratings: 96.14 percent for surface-level feedback, 93.03 percent for deep-level macro feedback, and 94.69 percent for deep-level micro feedback. An interactive user interface has been developed for the system and is publicly available and free to use.

AI.tldr: WrAFT is an automated writing evaluation tool for argumentative essays that provides accurate scoring and comprehensive feedback using large language models, achieving state-of-the-art performance on TOEFL essays.
AI.motivation: There is a need for automated writing evaluation tools that can deliver both accurate scoring and comprehensive feedback for argumentative essays, particularly for standardized tests like TOEFL.
AI.method: WrAFT uses a modular design with scoring, surface-level feedback, and deep-level feedback modules. It evaluates multiple LLMs (LLaMA-3.3-70B-Instruct, GPT-4o, Claude 3.7) using both direct prompting and supervised fine-tuning approaches on a dataset of 480 TOEFL Independent Writing essays.
AI.result: Achieved state-of-the-art scoring performance with QWK of 0.84 and RMSE of 0.44. Human evaluation showed high approval ratings: 96.14% for surface-level feedback, 93.03% for deep-level macro feedback, and 94.69% for deep-level micro feedback.
AI.conclusion: WrAFT is an effective automated writing assessment tool that provides both accurate scoring and comprehensive feedback, with a publicly available interactive interface for free use.

title: Muse: Representation Geometry of Muon Beyond Normalized Momentum
summary: Muon-style optimizers apply a polar map to matrix momentum, but their updates also depend on the representation of each parameter block before orthogonalization. We study this representation choice as a form of optimizer geometry and introduce {\method}, a family of Muon-style optimizers that shares the same momentum rule and Newton--Schulz backend across native, nearest-square, skinny, and vector representations. Each Frobenius-isometric representation induces a distinct polar steepest-descent geometry, in which the shorter matrix dimension determines the number of supported singular channels, the pullback scaling, and the constants in stochastic nonconvex convergence bounds. In a teacher--student model, curvature collapse and an isotropic Marchenko--Pastur spectral profile connect early-stage dissipation to the represented nuclear-to-squared-Frobenius norm ratio. Pretraining experiments on LLaMA2-130M and LLaMA2-600M, together with fixed-momentum diagnostics, show that balanced non-native representations can match the performance of the native representation, whereas reducing the shorter dimension weakens the scaling and singular-channel support, leading to behavior that increasingly resembles normalized momentum.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a family of Muon-style optimizers that share momentum rules across different matrix representations, showing how representation geometry affects optimization performance through singular channel support and scaling properties.
AI.motivation: Muon-style optimizers' updates depend on parameter block representation before orthogonalization, and different representations create distinct optimization geometries. The authors want to systematically study how representation choice affects optimizer behavior and performance.
AI.method: The authors introduce a family of Muon-style optimizers that share the same momentum rule and Newton-Schulz backend across four representations (native, nearest-square, skinny, vector). They analyze how Frobenius-isometric representations induce different polar steepest-descent geometries, where the shorter matrix dimension determines singular channel support and scaling properties.
AI.result: In experiments: 1) Balanced non-native representations match native representation performance in LLaMA2-130M and LLaMA2-600M pretraining, 2) Reducing the shorter dimension weakens scaling and singular-channel support, making behavior resemble normalized momentum, 3) Teacher-student models show curvature collapse and isotropic Marchenko-Pastur spectral profiles connecting early-stage dissipation to nuclear-to-squared-Frobenius norm ratios.
AI.conclusion: The representation choice in Muon-style optimizers acts as optimizer geometry, influencing singular channel support, scaling properties, and convergence behavior. Balanced representations can achieve similar performance to native ones, while dimension reduction affects optimization dynamics, making it increasingly similar to normalized momentum.

title: Are LLM-Generated GPU Kernels Production-Ready? A Trace-Driven Benchmark and Optimization Agent
comment: Both artifacts are released as open source: Atrex-Bench (https://github.com/alibaba/atrex-bench) and Atrex-Kernel-Agent (https://github.com/alibaba/atrex-kernel-agent)
summary: Existing GPU kernel generation benchmarks draw problems from synthetic or curated sources that diverge from deployed workloads. We present Atrex-Bench, a benchmark whose 30 operators and 440 shapes are sampled directly from full-cluster production inference traces of compute-limited, memory-rich GPUs. Each problem carries an importance weight derived from its share of observed GPU time, weighted by application card-hours and computed separately for the serving phases in which it runs, together with a per-problem roofline ceiling, so the aggregate score emphasizes the kernels that consume the most serving time. Evaluating six frontier coding agents on Atrex-Bench shows that even the best vanilla model reaches only ${\sim}10\%$ of the hardware roofline on production operators; and correctness alone overstates capability, since much of the apparent pass rate comes from PyTorch fallbacks rather than kernels the model wrote. To close this gap, we co-release Atrex-Kernel-Agent (AKA), a profile-driven kernel-optimization agent that combines iterative measure-revise search, optimization dropout for escaping stalled search contexts, and a layered GPU-optimization knowledge base (298 reference-kernel files and 244 optimization-knowledge documents, plus external upstream reference projects for API/ISA lookup). In a controlled case study, the agent converts zero-FlyDSL fallbacks into real kernels that match or exceed hand-tuned production baselines.

AI.tldr: Atrex-Bench introduces a production-sampled GPU kernel benchmark showing coding agents achieve only ~10% of hardware roofline, and Atrex-Kernel-Agent (AKA) uses profile-driven optimization to convert fallbacks into production-quality kernels.
AI.motivation: Existing GPU kernel generation benchmarks use synthetic or curated sources that don't reflect real deployed workloads, creating a gap between academic benchmarks and production reality where kernel performance directly impacts serving costs and efficiency.
AI.method: 1) Created Atrex-Bench by sampling 30 operators and 440 shapes directly from production inference traces of compute-limited, memory-rich GPUs, with importance weights based on GPU time share, application card-hours, and per-problem roofline ceilings. 2) Evaluated six frontier coding agents. 3) Developed Atrex-Kernel-Agent (AKA) featuring iterative measure-revise search, optimization dropout for escaping stalled contexts, and a layered GPU-optimization knowledge base (298 kernel files, 244 knowledge documents).
AI.result: Even best vanilla models reach only ~10% of hardware roofline on production operators; apparent pass rates are misleading as they come from PyTorch fallbacks rather than actual model-generated kernels. AKA successfully converts zero-FlyDSL fallbacks into real kernels that match or exceed hand-tuned production baselines in controlled case studies.
AI.conclusion: The gap between academic kernel generation benchmarks and production reality is substantial, requiring specialized agents like AKA that combine production-sampled evaluation with iterative, knowledge-driven optimization to achieve production-grade kernel performance.

title: CASP: Learning-Augmented Offline Approximation with Verifiable Certificates and Bounded-Loss PAC Guarantees
summary: Machine-learned predictions can speed up offline NP-hard optimization, but asking a predictor what to do amounts to asking it to solve the problem, and committing an unchecked prediction forfeits every worst-case guarantee. CASP (Certificate-Augmented Solution Pruning) instead asks which parts of the search space may be ignored, and accepts each answer only after a sound polynomial-time verifier has checked it, so correctness never depends on prediction quality. We develop the learning theory of this design. The verifier makes the induced loss class uniformly bounded, so certificate parameters are learnable from $\tilde O(\varepsilon^{-2}\log K)$ samples ($K$ the maximum instance size), whereas the unverified commitment class admits no distribution-free rate and, under cost spread $R$, none below $Ω(R/\varepsilon^2)$. Filtering noisy predictions by verifiable confidence dominates the standard min-combiner, with a margin we compute in closed form, and the prediction stays useful even given the LP, because it breaks ties on degenerate optimal faces, where every symmetric LP policy, meaning one whose commitments depend on the instance only through the verifiable confidence values, provably stalls. Experiments on five problems test the theory's quantitative predictions. With trained predictors, unverified pruning loses up to $26%$ of the optimum under distribution shift, while the verified deployment of the same predictions loses nothing.

AI.tldr: CASP is a framework that uses machine learning predictions to speed up NP-hard optimization while maintaining worst-case guarantees by verifying predictions with sound polynomial-time verifiers before pruning search space.
AI.motivation: Traditional use of ML predictions in NP-hard optimization either requires the predictor to solve the full problem or forfeits worst-case guarantees when committing to unchecked predictions. There's a need to leverage ML predictions safely without compromising correctness.
AI.method: Certificate-Augmented Solution Pruning (CASP): Instead of asking the predictor what to do, it asks which parts of the search space can be ignored. Each answer is checked by a sound polynomial-time verifier before acceptance, ensuring correctness regardless of prediction quality.
AI.result: Verifier makes loss class uniformly bounded, enabling learnability from O(ε⁻²log K) samples. Verified approach dominates unverified methods, maintains optimality even under distribution shift, and breaks ties on degenerate optimal faces where symmetric LP policies stall.
AI.conclusion: CASP provides a theoretically sound framework for using ML predictions in optimization while preserving worst-case guarantees. Verified deployment prevents performance degradation under distribution shift where unverified methods lose optimality.

title: Probabilistic Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Estimating Heterogeneous Elastic Properties from Low-Resolution and Noisy Displacement Data
summary: Estimating spatially heterogeneous elastic properties from low-resolution displacement measurements is a severely ill-posed inverse elasticity problem because low resolution obscures spatial details needed to distinguish heterogeneous property variations, and small measurement perturbations or fitting errors are amplified through inverse estimation. Existing inverse methods often rely on high-fidelity observations and manually prespecified loss weights, limiting their adaptability and making them sensitive to noise and resolution degradation. We propose a Probabilistic Inverse Elasticity Physics-Informed Neural Network (PIE-PINN) framework for robust estimation of Young's modulus and Poisson's ratio from noisy, low-resolution displacement data. PIE-PINN models displacement observation, strain-discrepancy, and equilibrium residuals using Laplace distributions within a unified probabilistic model. To improve robustness, the framework combines a B-spline-guided displacement network with a hierarchical half-Cauchy model for displacement residual scales. The B-spline provides a smooth global representation of the displacement field, while the neural network correction captures local variations. The hierarchical scale model adaptively downweights severe displacement fitting errors, enabling more robust recovery of the latent mean displacement field. An alternating maximum-likelihood training strategy updates the mean through weighted residual minimization and updates the scales to adjust the loss weights. Systematic case studies across varying noise levels and observation resolutions demonstrate the robustness of PIE-PINN.

AI.tldr: PIE-PINN is a probabilistic framework for robust estimation of elastic properties from noisy, low-resolution displacement data using physics-informed neural networks with adaptive weighting and B-spline guidance.
AI.motivation: Inverse elasticity estimation from low-resolution displacement measurements is severely ill-posed - low resolution obscures spatial details needed to distinguish heterogeneous properties, and small errors are amplified during inverse estimation. Existing methods rely on high-fidelity data and manual loss weights, limiting adaptability and making them sensitive to noise and resolution degradation.
AI.method: Proposed PIE-PINN framework models displacement observation, strain-discrepancy, and equilibrium residuals using Laplace distributions in a unified probabilistic model. Uses B-spline-guided displacement network for smooth global representation with neural network correction for local variations. Implements hierarchical half-Cauchy model for displacement residual scales to adaptively downweight severe fitting errors. Employs alternating maximum-likelihood training: updates mean through weighted residual minimization and updates scales to adjust loss weights.
AI.result: Systematic case studies across varying noise levels and observation resolutions demonstrate the robustness of PIE-PINN framework for estimating Young's modulus and Poisson's ratio from noisy, low-resolution displacement data.
AI.conclusion: PIE-PINN provides a robust probabilistic framework for ill-posed inverse elasticity problems, overcoming limitations of existing methods through adaptive error weighting and combined B-spline-neural network representation, enabling reliable property estimation from challenging low-resolution, noisy displacement measurements.

title: Seeing the End at Step Zero: Accelerating Diffusion MLLMs via MLP Sparsity-Aware Truncation
summary: Diffusion Multimodal Large Language Models (DMLLMs) are highly effective for multimodal reasoning, yet their inference efficiency is significantly hindered by fixed-length generation constraints. Since the actual output length is unknown, output sequences are padded to a predefined maximum length, resulting in substantial redundant computation over unnecessary [EOS] tokens. In this work, we discover that DMLLMs implicitly reveal their valid semantic boundary at the very first denoising step through a distinct shift in MLP activation sparsity. Leveraging this observation, we propose Seer, a training-free framework that detects this boundary using a Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)-based criterion and performs one-shot truncation of the redundant suffix for all subsequent computations. To preserve these theoretical gains during batched serving, Seer incorporates a hybrid execution strategy that maximizes throughput while seamlessly accommodating dynamic sequence lengths. Experimental results demonstrate that Seer effectively eliminates padding waste, accelerating throughput by up to $\sim$31$\times$. Across 9 benchmarks, Seer robustly maintains overall performance and even improves accuracy on complex visual tasks by mitigating noise leakage (e.g., DocVQA score increases from 63.52 to 63.66), offering a highly efficient, plug-and-play solution for DMLLM acceleration.

AI.tldr: Seer is a training-free framework that accelerates Diffusion Multimodal LLMs by detecting semantic boundaries early in denoising to eliminate padding waste, achieving up to 31× throughput speedup without performance loss.
AI.motivation: DMLLMs suffer from inefficient inference due to fixed-length generation constraints requiring padding sequences to maximum length, causing substantial redundant computation over unnecessary [EOS] tokens.
AI.method: Discovers that DMLLMs reveal valid semantic boundaries at the first denoising step via MLP activation sparsity shifts. Uses SNR-based criterion to detect boundaries and performs one-shot truncation of redundant suffixes. Implements hybrid execution strategy for batched serving.
AI.result: Seer accelerates throughput by up to ~31×, eliminates padding waste, maintains overall performance across 9 benchmarks, and even improves accuracy on complex visual tasks (e.g., DocVQA score increases from 63.52 to 63.66).
AI.conclusion: Seer offers a highly efficient, plug-and-play solution for DMLLM acceleration by leveraging early semantic boundary detection to eliminate redundant computation while maintaining or improving performance.

title: Gate-Zero Growth: A Geometric Framework for Function-Preserving Continual Learning
summary: We introduce \emph{gate-zero growth}, a function-preserving (FP) operator for continual learning that adds new residual blocks through a zero-initialised gate. Under a transversality condition, gate-zero growth induces \emph{rank separation} in the functional Jacobian: old directions are unchanged, new-weight directions are exactly flat at the growth point, and new gate directions are the only first-order source of new functional variation. As gates open during continual learning, function drift is $O(\|\boldsymbolα\|^2)$ and Jacobian leakage $O(\|\boldsymbolα\|_\infty)$, giving a controlled departure from the FP locus. On a $300\mathrm{M}\to857\mathrm{M}$ Transformer adapted from WikiText-103 to BookCorpus, gate-zero growth reaches near-zero old-domain forgetting ($Δ_A < 0.1$) under both exact-preservation (Isolation) and joint-frontier (Freeze-Nothing) operating points, while a non-FP control ($G_{\text{stack}}$) suffers an order-of-magnitude larger forgetting under the same recipe. The same geometric analysis covers LoRA, ReZero, and zero-init adapter constructions, establishing gate-zero growth as the canonical instance of a shared local geometry that governs safe capacity activation in CL.

AI.tldr: Gate-zero growth is a function-preserving method for continual learning that adds new residual blocks via zero-initialized gates, enabling controlled capacity expansion with minimal forgetting of old tasks.
AI.motivation: Continual learning faces the challenge of catastrophic forgetting when adapting models to new tasks while preserving performance on previously learned tasks. There's a need for methods that can safely expand model capacity without disrupting existing functionality.
AI.method: Introduces gate-zero growth, a function-preserving operator that adds new residual blocks through zero-initialized gates. The method ensures that at initialization, the expanded model behaves identically to the original. Under transversality conditions, it achieves rank separation in the functional Jacobian, keeping old directions unchanged while new parameters are initialized at flat critical points.
AI.result: On a 300M→857M Transformer adapted from WikiText-103 to BookCorpus, gate-zero growth achieves near-zero old-domain forgetting (Δ_A < 0.1) under both exact-preservation and joint-frontier settings, significantly outperforming non-function-preserving controls. The method enables safe capacity expansion while maintaining performance on previously learned tasks.
AI.conclusion: Gate-zero growth provides a principled geometric approach to continual learning that minimizes forgetting through controlled capacity expansion. The analysis reveals shared local geometry with existing methods like LoRA, ReZero, and zero-init adapters, establishing gate-zero growth as a canonical instance of safe capacity activation in continual learning.

title: Democratizing Agent Deployment Safety: A Structural Monitoring Approach
comment: Accepted in ICML 2026 Workshops: AI4GOOD and AIWILD
summary: AI software development agents are increasingly capable of modifying infrastructure and security critical systems, creating risks where an agent completes its assigned task while covertly weakening safeguards through actions such as broadening permissions, degrading logging, or introducing persistence mechanisms. While frontier laboratories may deploy sophisticated monitoring pipelines, many organizations and individual users adopting coding agents lack the resources and governance maturity required to maintain complex learned monitor ensembles. We study this problem as infrastructure level sabotage under task success in the infrastructure-as-code setting from ControlArena and compare trained and untrained monitors across synchronous and asynchronous deployment modes. We introduce an Information Flow Graph (IFG) monitor that analyzes structural security regressions using control-flow and data-flow graph diffs alongside raw code diffs. In asynchronous evaluation, an untrained git diff monitor misses 11.6% attacks at 1% false positive rate auditing budget, our untrained IFG monitor reduces this to 3.5%, and the trained Async RF monitor achieves below 1%. Unlike the trained Async RF monitor, IFG can also operate synchronously as a pre-deployment safeguard that blocks suspicious infrastructure changes before execution or merge. In synchronous mode, IFG realtime rollback reduces joint main task and covert task success from 74.4% to 0.0% with no measurable reduction in legitimate task completion. These results suggest that untrained structural monitors provide a practical and auditable path toward democratizing deployment safety for organizations seeking trustworthy adoption of advanced AI agents.

AI.tldr: An Information Flow Graph (IFG) monitor detects and prevents AI agent infrastructure sabotage by analyzing structural security regressions through control-flow and data-flow graph diffs, achieving near-perfect protection with minimal false positives.
AI.motivation: AI development agents can covertly weaken security safeguards while completing their tasks, creating risks for organizations that lack sophisticated monitoring pipelines. Frontier labs have resources for complex monitoring, but many organizations need practical, accessible security solutions for trustworthy AI agent adoption.
AI.method: Proposed an Information Flow Graph (IFG) monitor that analyzes structural security regressions using control-flow and data-flow graph diffs alongside raw code diffs. Compared trained and untrained monitors across synchronous and asynchronous deployment modes in the ControlArena infrastructure-as-code setting.
AI.result: In asynchronous mode: untrained git diff monitor missed 11.6% attacks at 1% false positive rate; untrained IFG reduced misses to 3.5%; trained Async RF achieved below 1%. In synchronous mode: IFG realtime rollback reduced joint task and covert task success from 74.4% to 0.0% with no reduction in legitimate task completion.
AI.conclusion: Untrained structural monitors like IFG provide a practical, auditable path for democratizing deployment safety, enabling organizations without advanced monitoring resources to adopt AI agents securely through structural analysis of infrastructure changes.

title: Sharp Stability Threshold and Certification for Designing Stable Residual Architectures
summary: We propose \emph{the sublinear-growth principle} for deep residual architectures -- a sharp stability threshold on the input-magnitude exponent of every residual block's velocity field: $$\|v(x, t)\| \leq c\,\|x\|^q + b, \qquad q \in [0, 1].$$ The threshold $q = 1$ is established via two independent arguments. Classical ODE theory gives a global forward flow on $[0, T]$ at $q \le 1$ and exhibits divergent velocity fields at any $q > 1$. The optimal-control analysis, via the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation, sharpens this to a selection statement: the training optimum is bang-bang on the boundary of the admissible class, so the optimum at $q > 1$ blows up while the optimum at $q \le 1$ is safe by construction. The exponent criterion $q \le 1$ is thereby a necessary and sufficient condition for stable training. It clarifies architectural placements that ensure the stability of training and inference, explaining, for instance, the stabilizing role of layer normalization. The sublinear-growth velocity fields form \emph{the right function space} on which forward dynamics, adjoint sensitivity, and architectural composition are all well-controlled. An arithmetic of input-magnitude exponents under the five operations that build residual blocks enables efficient certification of $q_k \le 1$ at the level of architectural primitives, in place of ad hoc trial and error in the search for stable neural architectural designs. A parameter-free modification reduces the supercritical Mamba block from $q = 5$ to $q = 1$ without layer normalization, demonstrating this point. Experiments on Mamba and PatchTST confirm that the $q \le 1$ variants train stably: the criterion is the input-magnitude exponent, not the presence of a normalization layer.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a "sublinear-growth principle" establishing q≤1 as a stability threshold for residual blocks' velocity fields, where ∥v(x,t)∥ ≤ c∥x∥^q + b, providing necessary and sufficient conditions for stable training.
AI.motivation: To establish a rigorous stability criterion for deep residual architectures that explains architectural stability issues and provides theoretical foundation for stable neural architecture design, moving beyond ad hoc trial-and-error approaches.
AI.method: Two independent theoretical approaches: (1) Classical ODE theory analyzing global forward flow, and (2) Optimal-control analysis via Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation to characterize training optima. An arithmetic of input-magnitude exponents enables certification at architectural primitive level.
AI.result: Established q≤1 as necessary and sufficient condition for stable training; demonstrated parameter-free modification reduces Mamba block from q=5 to q=1 without layer normalization; experiments on Mamba and PatchTST confirm stable training with q≤1 variants.
AI.conclusion: The sublinear-growth principle provides fundamental theoretical understanding of residual network stability, enables systematic architectural certification, and explains stabilization mechanisms like layer normalization through the q≤1 criterion rather than specific architectural elements.

title: Alipay-PIBench: A Realistic Payment Integration Benchmark for Coding Agents
summary: Payment integration is a demanding repository-level software task: agents must select a suitable product, implement coordinated client-server flows, verify payment outcomes, and preserve consistency between transaction and business states. We introduce Alipay-PIBench, a benchmark for evaluating coding agents on realistic Alipay payment integration. It contains nine product-specific projects and 18 task instances, each organized into Basic functional-completion and Advanced risk-aware hardening scenarios. Scenario-specific rubrics support deterministic static, unit, integration, and end-to-end checks, supplemented by LLM-assisted assessment for semantic requirements. We evaluate six coding-agent models and report rubric pass rate (RPR). Under the with-skill condition, mean RPR ranges from 68.58% to 91.37%. Access to the alipay-payment-integration skill improves mean RPR by 10.31 percentage points on average relative to the without-skill condition, with gains varying across models, products, and scenarios. Method-level results distinguish source-level completion, executable payment behavior, and payment-domain requirements. Alipay-PIBench provides a controlled setting for diagnosing model capability and evaluating structured guidance in payment integration.

AI.tldr: Alipay-PIBench is a new benchmark for evaluating coding agents on realistic Alipay payment integration tasks, featuring product-specific projects with basic and advanced scenarios, and specialized assessment rubrics.
AI.motivation: Payment integration is a complex repository-level software task requiring product selection, coordinated client-server flows, outcome verification, and state consistency. There's a need for specialized benchmarks to evaluate coding agents on realistic payment integration scenarios.
AI.method: The authors created Alipay-PIBench containing 9 product-specific projects and 18 task instances organized into Basic (functional-completion) and Advanced (risk-aware hardening) scenarios. They developed scenario-specific rubrics with deterministic static, unit, integration, and end-to-end checks, plus LLM-assisted semantic requirement assessment.
AI.result: Evaluation of six coding-agent models shows mean rubric pass rate (RPR) ranging from 68.58% to 91.37% under the with-skill condition. Access to the alipay-payment-integration skill improves mean RPR by 10.31 percentage points on average compared to without-skill. Method-level analysis distinguishes source-level completion, executable payment behavior, and payment-domain requirements.
AI.conclusion: Alipay-PIBench provides a controlled setting for diagnosing model capability and evaluating structured guidance in payment integration, with results demonstrating significant performance improvements when models have access to payment-specific skills.

title: Accelerating A/B-Tests with Counterfactual Estimation: Reducing Variance through Policy Overlap
summary:
Online controlled experiments are the gold standard for hypothesis testing in online platforms. Notwithstanding their ubiquity, they are notoriously expensive to run, and issues of variance hamper statistical power in assessing treatment effects. While standard variance reduction techniques leverage model-based control variates to reduce outcome noise, they remain agnostic to potential structural relationships between competing policies.
  In this work, we identify a critical inefficiency in the standard A/B-testing protocol: when a treatment and control policy agree on an action, the resulting outcome contributes noise but no signal regarding the treatment effect -- unnecessarily inflating confidence intervals. We propose a novel experimental protocol that exploits this policy overlap to accelerate experimentation. The key insight is to frame the randomised treatment assignment mechanism as a meta-policy, and leverage $Δ$-Off-Policy Estimation methods to obtain unbiased estimates for average treatment effects. We prove analytically that our approach recovers standard A/B-testing practices in the general case, but that its variance scales with the divergence between policies rather than raw outcome variance. Hence, we dominate the standard Difference-in-Means estimator whenever policies have common support, and the improvement is strict whenever the overlap region contributes non-zero residual variance. Empirical results corroborate these theoretical insights -- holding promise for significant impact on the real-world evaluation of recommender systems, information retrieval pipelines, and large language model interfaces.

AI.tldr: A novel experimental protocol that exploits policy overlap in A/B tests to accelerate experimentation by reducing variance when treatment and control policies agree on actions, using Δ-Off-Policy Estimation methods.
AI.motivation: Standard A/B tests are expensive and suffer from high variance. When treatment and control policies agree on actions, the resulting outcomes contribute noise but no signal about treatment effects, unnecessarily inflating confidence intervals and slowing down experimentation.
AI.method: Frames randomized treatment assignment as a meta-policy and leverages Δ-Off-Policy Estimation methods to obtain unbiased estimates of average treatment effects. The approach exploits policy overlap by recognizing that outcomes from actions where policies agree don't provide signal about treatment effects.
AI.result: The proposed approach recovers standard A/B-testing in general cases but reduces variance proportional to policy divergence rather than raw outcome variance. It dominates the standard Difference-in-Means estimator when policies have common support, with strict improvement when overlap regions contribute non-zero residual variance.
AI.conclusion: This method offers significant potential for accelerating online experimentation by reducing variance through exploiting policy overlap, promising major impact on evaluating recommender systems, information retrieval pipelines, and large language model interfaces.

title: Collaborative Spatial Learning with Multi-LLM Agents in Networked Social Experiments
comment: Accepted at ASONAM 2026; to appear in the Springer proceedings
summary: Collective problem solving often requires that group members consider the tradeoff between exploitation of known solutions and exploration for new ones, where information of known solutions can be disseminated among individual members through communication networks. The Mason--Watts experiment (PNAS 2012) showed that human groups in shorter-path networks outperform those in longer-path networks on a two-dimensional search task. In this work, we focus on the investigation of such network-efficiency effects in the setting of a group of large language model (LLM) agents. Specifically, we consider groups of sixteen LLM agents playing the Mason--Watts experiment on the eight Mason--Watts network topologies. Moreover, we develop mechanistic Bayesian optimization agents such that the performance of LLM agents can be compared with both the mechanistic agents and the human experimental data. Our computational experiments indicate that the LLM agents show a significant network-efficiency effect when instructed to randomize their first-round choices, but not under the default initialization. In this experiment, adding a one-sentence first-round randomization instruction improves collective payoff by more than three times the estimated payoff difference across the eight network topologies. Also, the Bayesian optimization agents obtain higher payoffs than the evaluated LLM agents on this spatial search task. We further compare the agents' exploration--exploitation behavior, copying, and spatial diversity.

AI.tldr: LLM agents show network efficiency effects in collective problem solving only when instructed to randomize initial choices, while Bayesian optimization agents outperform LLMs on spatial search tasks.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether network-efficiency effects observed in human groups (where shorter-path networks outperform longer-path ones in collective problem solving) also apply to groups of LLM agents, and to compare LLM performance with mechanistic agents and human data.
AI.method: Used groups of 16 LLM agents playing the Mason-Watts experiment on eight network topologies, with some agents receiving randomization instructions. Developed mechanistic Bayesian optimization agents as comparison baseline. Analyzed exploration-exploitation behavior, copying patterns, and spatial diversity.
AI.result: LLM agents showed significant network-efficiency effect only when instructed to randomize first-round choices (not under default initialization). Randomization instruction improved collective payoff by more than 3x the estimated payoff difference across networks. Bayesian optimization agents obtained higher payoffs than LLM agents on the spatial search task.
AI.conclusion: Instruction design significantly impacts LLM agents' collective performance in network-based problem solving. Simple behavioral modifications (like randomization instructions) can substantially improve outcomes. Bayesian optimization agents demonstrate superior performance in this spatial search context compared to current LLM agents.

title: Auditing Fairness-Privacy Trade-offs: Subpopulation-Level Effects of Fairness-Enhancing Algorithms
summary: Machine learning (ML) models deployed in sensitive domains such as healthcare, law enforcement, and finance must satisfy not only utility requirements but also fairness and privacy guarantees. While prior work has largely examined how privacy-preserving techniques affect fairness, the inverse question-how fairness-enhancing algorithms influence privacy leakage-remains underexplored. We present the first comprehensive study of how fairness interventions affect membership inference privacy risks at the subpopulation level. By adapting the Likelihood Ratio Attack (LiRA) for subgroup auditing, we uncover privacy disparities that aggregate evaluations obscure. We further analyze how Differential Privacy (DP) interacts with fairness-enhancing methods across different categories, showing that DP's privacy benefits and utility costs are unevenly distributed across subpopulations. Our results demonstrate that fairness interventions do not uniformly increase privacy risk; their impact depends on model architecture, subgroup size, and mitigation strategy. These findings reveal that fairness, privacy, and utility must be jointly evaluated at the subpopulation level, and we introduce the first unified empirical framework to support such auditing in practice.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how fairness interventions affect privacy risks at subpopulation level, finding privacy disparities obscured by aggregate metrics. It introduces a unified framework for joint evaluation of fairness, privacy, and utility.
AI.motivation: ML models in sensitive domains need both fairness and privacy guarantees. Prior work focused on how privacy affects fairness, but the inverse question—how fairness algorithms affect privacy—remains underexplored, especially at subpopulation level.
AI.method: Adapts Likelihood Ratio Attack (LiRA) for subgroup privacy auditing. Analyzes interactions between Differential Privacy and fairness-enhancing methods across different categories. Examines how model architecture, subgroup size, and mitigation strategies affect privacy risks.
AI.result: Fairness interventions don't uniformly increase privacy risk—their impact varies by model architecture, subgroup size, and mitigation strategy. DP's privacy benefits and utility costs are unevenly distributed across subpopulations. Aggregate evaluations obscure important privacy disparities.
AI.conclusion: Fairness, privacy, and utility must be jointly evaluated at subpopulation level. The paper introduces the first unified empirical framework for such auditing in practice, revealing complex interactions between fairness interventions and privacy risks.

title: Multi-LLM Collaborative MRI Report Generation for Visual Instruction Tuning in Brain Oncology
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) and their extension to vision-language models (VLMs) have made it easier to combine text and images for tasks such as report generation. Existing VLMs in medicine typically focus on 2D images (chest X-rays), and their extension to 3D imaging has been difficult because of the lack of paired 3D imaging-text data. Thus, we introduce a new method for creating a 3D image-text dataset for brain oncology using 3D MRI scans of glioma and meningioma cases. We use a cooperative system in which several LLMs work together to generate and check reports, ensuring that they are accurate and clear. By leveraging the new 3D MRI-text dataset, we further build a VLM that converts MRI scans into tokens and aligns them with text instructions. Our VLM performed better in report generation and visual question answering tasks than other 2D and 3D methods. Our method not only improves the quality of reports but also helps with better diagnosis and treatment in brain oncology.

AI.tldr: A method for creating 3D MRI-text datasets for brain oncology using cooperative LLMs to generate reports, enabling development of a vision-language model that outperforms existing 2D/3D methods in medical report generation and visual question answering.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-language models in medicine primarily focus on 2D images (like chest X-rays), and extending to 3D imaging has been challenging due to the lack of paired 3D imaging-text data, particularly for brain oncology applications.
AI.method: 1) Created a new 3D MRI-text dataset for brain oncology (glioma and meningioma cases) using a cooperative system where multiple LLMs work together to generate and verify reports for accuracy and clarity. 2) Built a vision-language model that converts 3D MRI scans into tokens and aligns them with text instructions using this dataset.
AI.result: The developed vision-language model outperformed other 2D and 3D methods in both report generation and visual question answering tasks for brain oncology applications.
AI.conclusion: The approach not only improves medical report quality but also contributes to better diagnosis and treatment in brain oncology by enabling effective 3D vision-language modeling for medical imaging.

title: Angular Gaussian Supervised Contrastive Learning for Long-Tailed Electrocardiogram Arrhythmia Diagnosis
summary: Long-tailed label distributions reduce the reliability of deep learning for electrocardiogram (ECG) arrhythmia diagnosis, particularly for clinically important but rare abnormalities. Existing rebalancing and logit adjustment methods mainly address class frequency while overlooking direction-dependent morphological variability across ECG classes. This study proposes Angular Gaussian Supervised Contrastive Learning (AG-SCL) for long-tailed multi-label ECG diagnosis. AG-SCL integrates three components into a unified framework: an Angular Gaussian contrastive branch that models full-covariance class uncertainty on unit-normalized embeddings, Adaptive Logit Adjustment that learns bounded label-state-specific prior corrections instead of fixed frequency-based margins, and tail-aware augmentation that generates morphology-preserving views while protecting the 7-25 Hz QRS-dominant band. The method was evaluated on the public PTB-XL benchmark and a nocturnal ECG dataset comprising 1317 hours of recordings from 141 subjects. AG-SCL achieved the best macro-level performance on both datasets. On PTB-XL, it obtained a balanced accuracy of 0.838, sensitivity of 0.709, specificity of 0.968, mean average precision of 0.495, and TPR at 5% FPR of 0.778. On Noc-ECG, the corresponding values were 0.918, 0.889, 0.947, 0.488, and 0.900. The largest gains occurred in rare or morphologically unstable rhythm classes, while ablation studies confirmed the contributions of full-covariance modelling, Adaptive Logit Adjustment, and tail-aware augmentation. AG-SCL improves long-tailed ECG diagnosis by combining prior calibration with anisotropic representation learning, enhancing sensitivity to rare arrhythmias while maintaining clinically relevant specificity. Our code is available at: https://github.com/Open-EXG/AG-SCL-for-Long-Tailed-ECG.

AI.tldr: AG-SCL is a new method for long-tailed ECG arrhythmia diagnosis that combines angular Gaussian contrastive learning with adaptive logit adjustment and tail-aware augmentation to improve performance on rare cardiac abnormalities.
AI.motivation: Long-tailed label distributions in ECG datasets reduce reliability of deep learning for arrhythmia diagnosis, especially for clinically important but rare abnormalities. Existing methods focus on class frequency but overlook direction-dependent morphological variability across ECG classes.
AI.method: AG-SCL integrates three components: 1) Angular Gaussian contrastive branch that models full-covariance class uncertainty on unit-normalized embeddings, 2) Adaptive Logit Adjustment that learns bounded label-state-specific prior corrections instead of fixed frequency-based margins, and 3) tail-aware augmentation that generates morphology-preserving views while protecting the 7-25 Hz QRS-dominant band.
AI.result: On PTB-XL benchmark: balanced accuracy 0.838, sensitivity 0.709, specificity 0.968, mean average precision 0.495, TPR at 5% FPR 0.778. On Noc-ECG dataset (1317 hours from 141 subjects): balanced accuracy 0.918, sensitivity 0.889, specificity 0.947, mean average precision 0.488, TPR at 5% FPR 0.900. Largest gains in rare or morphologically unstable rhythm classes.
AI.conclusion: AG-SCL improves long-tailed ECG diagnosis by combining prior calibration with anisotropic representation learning, enhancing sensitivity to rare arrhythmias while maintaining clinically relevant specificity. The method effectively addresses both class frequency imbalance and morphological variability.

title: MathCoPilot: An Interactive System for Human-AI Symbiotic Paradigm of Mathematical Research
summary: Existing LLM-based theorem provers have achieved impressive results on formal mathematics benchmarks, yet they remain confined to acting as autonomous agents that prove a stated proposition. In this paper, we propose MathCoPilot, a human-in-the-loop system that embodies a new human--AI symbiotic paradigm for mathematical research, in which the mathematician steers the high-level mathematical direction while AI agents carry out the detailed formalization and proof work under continuous human guidance. MathCoPilot unifies three core capabilities: (1) an interactive workbench where the mathematician and AI agents collaborate through a living proof blueprint that decomposes a proof into navigable steps the human can directly inspect, direct, and refine; (2) automated proving skill orchestration with adaptive knowledge base search and Lean-integrated iterative verification; and (3) topic-driven paper retrieval and automated formalization into a verified Lean knowledge base. Using MathCoPilot, we systematically compare four state-of-the-art LLMs, including Gemini~3.1~Pro, GPT-5.4, and Claude~Opus~4.7, on a FormalMATH subset and on two real PDE theorems requiring deep domain expertise, evaluating their ability to produce verified Lean~4 proofs and to identify errors in deliberately incorrect proofs. Our results show that while current models can handle undergraduate-level problems with high success rates under favorable autoformalization conditions, substantial challenges remain for domain-specific theorems requiring genuine mathematical understanding.

AI.tldr: MathCoPilot is a human-in-the-loop system for mathematical research where mathematicians provide high-level direction while AI agents handle formalization and proof work, featuring interactive collaboration, automated proving, and paper formalization capabilities.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based theorem provers are limited to acting as autonomous agents proving stated propositions, lacking true human-AI collaboration where mathematicians can steer high-level mathematical direction while AI handles detailed formalization work.
AI.method: MathCoPilot combines three core capabilities: (1) interactive workbench with living proof blueprint for human-AI collaboration, (2) automated proving skill orchestration with knowledge base search and Lean verification, and (3) topic-driven paper retrieval with automated formalization into verified Lean knowledge base.
AI.result: The system was used to evaluate four state-of-the-art LLMs (Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.7, and others) on FormalMATH and two real PDE theorems. Models could handle undergraduate-level problems with high success under favorable autoformalization, but struggled with domain-specific theorems requiring genuine mathematical understanding.
AI.conclusion: MathCoPilot demonstrates a new human-AI symbiotic paradigm for mathematical research, showing that while current LLMs can handle routine formalization tasks, substantial challenges remain for theorems requiring deep mathematical expertise, highlighting the need for continued advancement in AI mathematical reasoning.

title: Beyond Entropy: Correctness-Aware Advantage Shaping via Contrastive Policy Optimization
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) commonly uses entropy for advantage shaping. However, entropy cannot distinguish useful uncertainty from detrimental confusion, limiting its effectiveness as a correctness signal. We propose Contrastive Policy Optimization (CPO), which uses token-level contrastive disagreement between reference-guided and vanilla generation distributions for correctness-aware advantage shaping. Both theoretical and empirical results show that this disagreement reliably indicates token-level correctness. We further show that On-policy Distillation is a special case of CPO, where the posterior distribution is instantiated by an external teacher model. CPO also resolves the zero-advantage problem. Experiments on in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks demonstrate that CPO substantially outperforms entropy-based RLVR methods while maintaining strong generalization. Further analysis shows that correct and incorrect responses naturally support exploration and exploitation respectively, and balancing both leads to the best performance.

AI.tldr: CPO replaces entropy-based advantage shaping in RLVR with token-level contrastive disagreement between reference-guided and vanilla generation distributions for better correctness signals.
AI.motivation: Entropy in RLVR cannot distinguish useful uncertainty from detrimental confusion, limiting its effectiveness as a correctness signal. Need a more reliable token-level correctness indicator for advantage shaping.
AI.method: Contrastive Policy Optimization (CPO) uses token-level contrastive disagreement between reference-guided and vanilla generation distributions for correctness-aware advantage shaping, revealing that On-policy Distillation is a special case of CPO with external teacher models.
AI.result: CPO substantially outperforms entropy-based RLVR methods on in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks while maintaining strong generalization, resolves the zero-advantage problem, and shows correct/incorrect responses naturally support exploration/exploitation respectively.
AI.conclusion: CPO provides a more effective approach to RLVR by using contrastive disagreement as a reliable token-level correctness signal, offering both theoretical justification and empirical improvements over entropy-based methods.

title: SportD: Can VLMs Physically Strategize?
summary: Vision--language models have become increasingly capable of interpreting visual scenes, but it remains unclear whether they can use information to make strategically effective decisions. We investigate this question in soccer, where models observe the seconds preceding an on-ball decision and must choose whether to shoot or pass to a specific teammate. Unlike conventional visual-understanding tasks, soccer enables decisions to be evaluated quantitatively by estimating the value of every available action. We introduce SportD, a benchmark comprising 478 on-ball decisions from the 2022 FIFA World Cup. Each model choice is evaluated against a possession-value model that estimates the action that most increases the attacking team's probability of scoring, allowing us to measure both optimal-action accuracy and the value forfeited by suboptimal decisions. Across three frontier VLMs, the best selects the highest-valued action on 31.4% of events, compared with 38.9% for the professional players, and all models incur significantly greater regret. Further analysis reveals a systematic preference for lower-variance and lower-reward actions: VLMs shoot less often and select substantially less progressive passes than either the optimal policy or the real players. The models also reproduce the player's specific action above chance even when that action is suboptimal, suggesting partial imitation of familiar play patterns rather than consistent evaluation of counterfactual alternatives. SportD provides a value-grounded testbed for measuring physical strategic reasoning in VLMs.

AI.tldr: VLMs struggle with strategic soccer decisions, performing worse than professional players and showing systematic biases toward low-risk, low-reward actions rather than optimal value-based choices.
AI.motivation: While vision-language models excel at visual understanding, their ability to make strategically effective decisions using visual information remains unclear. The authors investigate this in soccer, which provides a quantitative way to evaluate decisions through possession-value modeling.
AI.method: The authors introduce SportD, a benchmark of 478 on-ball decisions from the 2022 FIFA World Cup. Models observe seconds preceding decisions and must choose whether to shoot or pass. Decisions are evaluated against a possession-value model that estimates which action most increases the attacking team's scoring probability.
AI.result: The best VLM selects the highest-valued action in only 31.4% of events vs 38.9% for professional players. VLMs incur significantly greater regret, show systematic preference for lower-variance/lower-reward actions (shoot less, select less progressive passes), and partially imitate player actions even when suboptimal.
AI.conclusion: VLMs demonstrate partial imitation of familiar play patterns rather than consistent evaluation of counterfactual alternatives. SportD provides a value-grounded testbed for measuring physical strategic reasoning in VLMs, revealing significant gaps in their decision-making capabilities.

title: PolyQ: Codesigning End-to-End Quantization Framework for Scalable Edge CPU LLM Inference
summary: CPUs are the most universal target for on-device LLM inference, but existing low-bit quantization methods offer either coarse operating points or fine-grained mixed precision that is difficult to execute efficiently on CPUs. We present PolyQ, a CPU-oriented compiler/quantization co-design for activation-aware channel-wise bit allocation under a user-specified average-bit budget. PolyQ assigns per-channel bit-widths from $\{2,3,4,8,16\}$, then uses a compile-time model compiler to permute and cluster channels into bit-homogeneous blocks, generate SIMD- and LUT-compatible kernels, and merge compatible permutations across operators to keep layout regularization off the runtime path. This turns fine-grained budget fitting into a practical fractional-bit deployment method for CPU-only inference. Across Falcon-H1-3B, Llama2-13B, and Qwen3-32B on WikiText-2, PolyQ provides stable quality scaling from 3--6\,b and improves perplexity by 2.4--32.1\% over prior methods at a 3\,b target. End-to-end measurements on three representative CPUs -- workstation, laptop, and mobile -- show that compiler layout regularization reduces activation reorder traffic by up to 70.8\%, prefill latency and decode throughput scale nearly proportionally with the configured bit budget, and energy/token overhead stays below 2\% relative to an optimized LUT-based back-end. These results show that fractional-bit CPU deployment is practical, predictable, and energy-efficient across diverse edge targets.

AI.tldr: PolyQ is a CPU-oriented compiler/quantization co-design that enables practical fractional-bit deployment for LLM inference on CPUs by combining activation-aware channel-wise bit allocation with compile-time layout optimization.
AI.motivation: CPUs are the most universal target for on-device LLM inference, but existing low-bit quantization methods suffer from limitations: they offer either coarse operating points or fine-grained mixed precision that is difficult to execute efficiently on CPUs, creating a gap between precise bit allocation and practical deployment.
AI.method: PolyQ features a compiler/quantization co-design with activation-aware channel-wise bit allocation. It assigns per-channel bit-widths from {2,3,4,8,16}, then uses a compile-time model compiler to permute and cluster channels into bit-homogeneous blocks, generate SIMD- and LUT-compatible kernels, and merge compatible permutations across operators to keep layout regularization off the runtime path.
AI.result: Across Falcon-H1-3B, Llama2-13B, and Qwen3-32B on WikiText-2, PolyQ provides stable quality scaling from 3-6 bits and improves perplexity by 2.4-32.1% over prior methods at 3-bit target. On three CPU platforms (workstation, laptop, mobile), compiler layout regularization reduces activation reorder traffic by up to 70.8%, prefill latency and decode throughput scale nearly proportionally with bit budget, and energy/token overhead stays below 2% relative to optimized LUT-based backend.
AI.conclusion: Fractional-bit CPU deployment for LLM inference is practical, predictable, and energy-efficient across diverse edge targets when using the PolyQ co-design approach, which bridges the gap between fine-grained quantization precision and efficient CPU execution.

title: Action QFormer: Structured Representation Shaping under Action Supervision in Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Action supervision in vision-language-action (VLA) models is often treated as a downstream objective for learning action prediction. In this paper, we study it instead as a force that shapes inherited multimodal representations. We show that this shaping has a dual effect: it is necessary for forming action-compatible representations, but when action supervision is applied too directly to the inherited multimodal pathway, it can also destabilize representations that support language-side processing and object grounding. To address this tension, we introduce Action QFormer, a query-based action-facing interface that uses instruction-conditioned queries to reorganize inherited multimodal information into action-facing representations before downstream action generation. In zero-shot sim-to-real navigation, Action QFormer improves average closed-loop task success from 18.8% to 56.3%, raises fixed-instruction action-generation correctness from 22.5% to 75.5%, and nearly eliminates out-of-distribution instruction generations. Further analyses show that Action QFormer changes how action supervision shapes inherited multimodal representations, reducing broad upstream rewriting while preserving targeted and sometimes constructive action-supervised adaptation. These results suggest that improving VLA performance requires not only stronger pretrained backbones, but also better ways of selecting and organizing inherited multimodal information while controlling how it is shaped under action supervision.

AI.tldr: Action QFormer introduces a query-based interface to selectively reorganize inherited multimodal representations for action generation, avoiding destabilization of language/object grounding while enabling effective action-compatible representations.
AI.motivation: Action supervision in VLA models has a dual effect: it's necessary for forming action-compatible representations, but when applied too directly to inherited multimodal pathways, it can destabilize representations that support language processing and object grounding. This creates a tension between maintaining useful inherited knowledge and enabling action prediction.
AI.method: Introduces Action QFormer, a query-based action-facing interface that uses instruction-conditioned queries to reorganize inherited multimodal information into action-facing representations before downstream action generation. This approach selectively shapes representations rather than broadly rewriting them.
AI.result: In zero-shot sim-to-real navigation: improves average closed-loop task success from 18.8% to 56.3%; raises fixed-instruction action-generation correctness from 22.5% to 75.5%; nearly eliminates out-of-distribution instruction generations. Analysis shows reduced broad upstream rewriting while preserving targeted action-supervised adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Improving VLA performance requires not only stronger pretrained backbones, but also better ways of selecting/organizing inherited multimodal information while controlling how it's shaped under action supervision. Action QFormer provides a query-based mechanism to address this representation-shaping challenge.

title: TIDE: Trustworthy and Interpretable Battery Degradation Estimation with Contextual Learning and Symbolic Distillation
summary: Battery health estimation is fundamental for battery management in battery-powered systems, where inaccurate health states may affect control, maintenance, and service life. It becomes even more critical in intelligent connected systems, where estimation errors can propagate across interconnected devices and downstream decisions. In this paper, we propose TIDE, a trustworthy and interpretable battery degradation estimator for reliable battery health estimation. TIDE jointly considers accuracy, trustworthiness, and interpretability, which are all essential for practical deployment and downstream decision making. To realize these objectives, TIDE combines battery-domain knowledge with operational measurements in a three-component backbone. A knowledge-guided degradation prior promotes trustworthy estimation, a monotone residual component provides interpretable aging-consistent refinement, and a contextual learning component captures battery-specific operational effects for improved accuracy. The trained backbone is then distilled into a compact symbolic surrogate that provides a concise model-level interpretation of its learned estimation logic. Experiments show that TIDE achieves strong estimation accuracy, improving overall estimation fidelity by an average of 19.7% over representative baselines. Its knowledge-guided prior and monotone residual modelling substantially reduce aging-consistency violations, supporting trustworthy estimation. Meanwhile, the backbone enables component-level interpretation, while symbolic distillation provides a compact model-level representation of the learned estimation logic. These results support the practical use of TIDE for battery health monitoring and decision support in intelligent connected systems.

AI.tldr: TIDE: A trustworthy and interpretable battery degradation estimator that combines battery-domain knowledge with operational measurements for reliable battery health estimation in intelligent connected systems.
AI.motivation: Accurate battery health estimation is critical for battery management systems, especially in intelligent connected systems where estimation errors can propagate across interconnected devices and affect downstream decisions. Current methods often lack trustworthiness and interpretability, which are essential for practical deployment.
AI.method: TIDE uses a three-component backbone: 1) knowledge-guided degradation prior for trustworthy estimation, 2) monotone residual component for interpretable aging-consistent refinement, and 3) contextual learning component to capture battery-specific operational effects. The trained model is distilled into a compact symbolic surrogate for model-level interpretation.
AI.result: TIDE achieves strong estimation accuracy, improving overall estimation fidelity by 19.7% over representative baselines. It substantially reduces aging-consistency violations and provides component-level interpretation through its backbone structure, plus model-level interpretation through symbolic distillation.
AI.conclusion: TIDE demonstrates practical value for battery health monitoring and decision support in intelligent connected systems by jointly addressing accuracy, trustworthiness, and interpretability requirements for reliable deployment.

title: Analytic Abduction: Causal Decomposition and Governed Commitment for Human--AI Coordination
summary: Abductive reasoning operates in two directions. The synthetic mode builds explanations from available hypotheses; the analytic mode, conversely, identifies the latent factors whose interaction accounts for a complex observed state. This paper develops the analytic mode as a non-greedy, risk-sensitive discipline of commitment, in which candidate factors coexist and interact, resolving into committed conclusions only when explicit governance conditions are met.
