AI.method: Proposes LLM-based detection system with two operating modes: 1) global mode classifies complete Reddit threads by combining local chunk-level assessments, 2) early warning mode processes threads sequentially and issues warnings when sliding window exceeds calibrated thresholds for three firestorm indicators (negativity share, escalation level, contributor count).
AI.result: On balanced Reddit dataset, global mode achieves strong classification performance, while early warning mode reaches high recall and detects escalating threads after only small number of comments and distinct contributors.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can be used not only for static judgment tasks but also as repeated estimators in context-aware monitoring of social media discourse, effectively detecting online firestorms.

title: Leveraging Instruction Tuning and Merging for Reasoning Model Adaptation
summary: Reasoning language models (RLMs) have demonstrated impressive performance in domains such as mathematics and coding. These domains permit reliable verification of model outputs, which is important for enabling the reinforcement learning that drives RLM performance gains. However, training RLMs on domains that lack reliable verifiers remains challenging. Meanwhile, for both verifiable and unverifiable domains, large amounts of unused supervised fine-tuning data with human-written solutions exist. In this work, we show that these data can be used efficiently to further improve RLM performance. For this, we first use classic instruction tuning, supervised fine-tuning without reasoning traces, on the RLM. Next, we merge our instruction-tuned model with the original reasoning model, recovering its reasoning behavior on the target domain. Our extensive evaluation demonstrates that our technique improves RLM performance in both verifiable and hard-to-verify domains, including coding and text summarization, while preserving RLM capabilities across other domains. Importantly, our method is highly cost-effective, enabling such improvements for less than USD $3.

AI.tldr: Instruction tuning combined with model merging improves reasoning language model performance in both verifiable and unverifiable domains while preserving existing capabilities at low cost.
AI.motivation: Reasoning language models (RLMs) perform well in verifiable domains (math, coding) but struggle in domains without reliable verification methods. There are large amounts of unused supervised fine-tuning data with human-written solutions that could potentially improve RLM performance across both verifiable and unverifiable domains.
AI.method: First perform instruction tuning (supervised fine-tuning without reasoning traces) on the RLM using available supervised data. Then merge the instruction-tuned model with the original reasoning model to recover reasoning behavior on the target domain while incorporating supervised learning benefits.
AI.result: The technique improves RLM performance in both verifiable domains (like coding) and hard-to-verify domains (like text summarization) while preserving RLM capabilities across other domains. The method is highly cost-effective, enabling improvements for less than $3.
AI.conclusion: Combining instruction tuning with model merging provides an efficient, low-cost method to enhance reasoning language models across diverse domains, including those without reliable verification mechanisms, while maintaining existing reasoning capabilities.

title: Explaining Process Control Optimisation Recommendations via GradientSHAP and Implicit Differentiation
summary: Automated optimisation is increasingly adopted in industrial processes, yet a trust gap persists between engineers who design these algorithms and operators who must act on their recommendations. Explainable AI methods like SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) have transformed interpretability for machine learning predictions; optimisation outputs could benefit from similar techniques. We present an approach that integrates Implicit Function Theorem (IFT) based sensitivity analysis with SHAP attribution and narrative generation via Large Language Models (LLM), producing explanations tailored for operators. Our approach leverages IFT to compute exact parameter sensitivities $\partial p^*/\partial x$ from the optimality conditions, enabling efficient GradientSHAP computation. For an industrial High Pressure Grinding Roll (HPGR) control optimisation problem with 22 features, we achieve equivalent SHAP attributions (correlation $>$0.99 with KernelSHAP) with over 40$\times$ speedup, enabling real-time natural language explanations. We validate on industrial scenarios and present feedback from domain experts on generated explanations.

AI.tldr: Integrates Implicit Function Theorem sensitivity analysis with SHAP and LLMs to generate real-time natural language explanations for optimization results, bridging trust gap between algorithm designers and operators.
AI.motivation: Automated optimization is widely adopted in industry but faces a trust gap between engineers designing algorithms and operators implementing recommendations. While Explainable AI methods like SHAP improve ML prediction interpretability, optimization outputs lack similar explainability techniques.
AI.method: Combines Implicit Function Theorem (IFT) based sensitivity analysis with SHAP attribution and Large Language Model narrative generation. Uses IFT to compute exact parameter sensitivities from optimality conditions, enabling efficient GradientSHAP computation for optimization problems.
AI.result: Applied to industrial High Pressure Grinding Roll control optimization with 22 features. Achieved equivalent SHAP attributions (correlation >0.99 with KernelSHAP) with over 40x speedup, enabling real-time natural language explanations. Validated on industrial scenarios with expert feedback.
AI.conclusion: The integrated approach successfully bridges the trust gap in industrial optimization by providing efficient, interpretable explanations tailored for operators, combining mathematical rigor with natural language communication.

title: Random Logit Scaling: Defending Deep Neural Networks Against Black-Box Score-Based Adversarial Example Attacks
comment: Accepted at Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR)
summary: Machine learning models are increasingly adapted in various domains. However, adversarial examples pose a significant threat to the reliable deployment of these models. In recent years, some powerful adversarial example attacks have been proposed for the fast and query-efficient generation of adversarial examples, even in black-box scenarios, highlighting the need for scalable, low-cost, and powerful defenses. In this work, we present two contributions to the domain of black-box adversarial example attacks and defenses. First, we propose Random Logit Scaling (RLS), a randomization-based defense against black-box score-based adversarial example attacks. RLS is a plug-and-play, post-processing defense that can be implemented on top of any existing ML model with minimal effort. The idea behind RLS is to confuse an attacker by outputting falsified scores resulting from randomly scaled logits while maintaining the model accuracy. We show that RLS significantly reduces the success rate of state-of-the-art black-box score-based attacks while preserving the accuracy and minimizing confidence score distortion compared to state-of-the-art randomization-based defenses. Second, we introduce a novel adaptive attack against AAA, a SOTA non-randomized black-box defense against black-box score-based attacks that also modifies output logits to confuse attackers, demonstrating its vulnerability against adaptive attacks.

AI.tldr: Random Logit Scaling (RLS) is a plug-and-play defense that randomizes output logits to confuse black-box attacks, while a novel adaptive attack is shown to break AAA defense.
AI.motivation: Adversarial examples threaten reliable ML deployment. While powerful black-box attacks exist, defenses need to be scalable, low-cost, and effective against these attacks.
AI.method: 1) RLS: Randomly scales logits during inference to confuse attackers while maintaining accuracy. 2) Adaptive attack against AAA: Develops an attack that adapts to logit-modifying defenses.
AI.result: RLS significantly reduces success rate of state-of-the-art black-box attacks while preserving accuracy and minimizing confidence distortion compared to other randomization defenses. The adaptive attack successfully breaks the AAA defense.
AI.conclusion: RLS offers an effective, easy-to-implement defense against black-box attacks, while demonstrating that even non-randomized defenses like AAA remain vulnerable to well-designed adaptive attacks.

title: CFM-Bench: A Unified Multi-Domain, Multi-Task Benchmark for Channel Foundation Models
summary: Channel foundation models (CFMs) are developing rapidly, with recent studies reporting benefits from pretraining across downstream wireless tasks. Yet CFMs are commonly evaluated in model-specific pipelines with different data, radio configurations, partitions, adaptation procedures, task definitions, and metrics. Reported comparisons therefore tend to show that pretraining improves over supervised training from scratch within one pipeline, but neither rank CFMs nor compare them fairly with task-specific models. We release CFM-Bench, a unified multi-domain, multi-task benchmark designed to address this gap. It curates six channel configurations spanning 3GPP statistical simulation, two independent ray-tracing pipelines, industrial and aerial measurements, and synchronized vehicular multimodal simulation. Official partitions isolate complete trajectories, measurement sessions, vehicle links, simulation realizations, or buffered spatial regions. CFM-Bench does not prescribe an external pretraining corpus or strategy; no benchmark split may be used for foundation-model pretraining, and the official training split is reserved exclusively for downstream fine-tuning. The benchmark additionally requires disclosure of all data used during model development and prohibits training-stage use of official test units. Six task groups are organized along three CFM application dimensions: physical-layer (PHY) channel intelligence, radio-access-network (RAN) decision intelligence, and integrated sensing and communication (ISAC). They cover CSI feedback, frequency and temporal channel extrapolation, propagation-state classification, current- and future-beam prediction, and single-frame and temporal localization. CFM-Bench provides a common substrate for comparing the transferability of channel representations across models, domains, and tasks.

AI.tldr: CFM-Bench is a unified multi-domain, multi-task benchmark for evaluating and comparing Channel Foundation Models across diverse wireless channel configurations, tasks, and domains with standardized evaluation protocols.
AI.motivation: Current CFM evaluations are fragmented across different pipelines with varying data, configurations, and metrics, preventing fair comparison between CFMs and with task-specific models. There's no standardized way to rank CFMs or measure their transferability across domains.
AI.method: The benchmark curates six diverse channel configurations spanning different sources (3GPP simulation, ray-tracing, measurements) with official partitions to isolate complete trajectories/sessions. It prohibits using benchmark data for pretraining and requires disclosure of all development data. Six task groups cover PHY channel intelligence, RAN decision intelligence, and ISAC applications.
AI.result: CFM-Bench provides a standardized framework with curated datasets across multiple domains, strict evaluation protocols, and comprehensive task coverage to enable fair comparison of CFM transferability across models, domains, and wireless tasks.
AI.conclusion: CFM-Bench addresses the critical gap in CFM evaluation by establishing a unified benchmark that enables systematic assessment and ranking of Channel Foundation Models' performance and transfer capabilities across diverse wireless scenarios and applications.

title: A Minimal Interpretable Architecture for Zero-Shot Reconstruction of Dynamical Systems
summary: Recent foundation models (FMs) for zero-shot reconstruction of dynamical systems (DS) achieve strong out-of-domain generalization but provide little insight into the mechanisms that underlie their forecasts. Such an understanding could help to strip down overladen FM architectures to their bare essence and expose the minimal requirements for in-context learning in the DS domain. Toward this goal, here we iteratively reduce a recent powerful SOTA model for DS reconstruction, DynaMix (Hemmer & Durstewitz, 2025), to a minimal interpretable two-parameter form, which we call DynaBase. DynaBase produces forecasts through a linear blend of the current latent state and the nearest in-context neighbor and its temporal successor. Surprisingly, despite its extreme simplicity, DynaBase produces highly competitive zero-shot DS reconstructions across chaotic and cyclic systems, with a negligible parameter load, many orders of magnitude below that of other FMs. Even more, this extreme simplicity permits direct model optimization on DS reconstruction measures, as well as closed-form one-step analytical solutions on prediction MSE. Theoretical and empirical analysis of DynaBase further leads to a 1-parameter family of maps, with the context-parroting algorithm of (Zhang & Gilpin, 2026) recovered at one end, and chaotic (divergent but bounded) behavior at the other. We further show how different training strategies lead to models either optimal for short-term prediction or for DS reconstruction. Thus, DynaBase not only exposes the minimal mechanisms required for producing zero-shot DS reconstruction, but also reconciles within an accessible mathematical frame divergent observations in the literature.

AI.tldr: Researchers create DynaBase, a minimal 2-parameter model for zero-shot dynamical system reconstruction, achieving competitive performance with extreme simplicity and revealing fundamental mechanisms of foundation models.
AI.motivation: Current foundation models for dynamical system reconstruction achieve strong out-of-domain generalization but lack interpretability, making it difficult to understand the underlying mechanisms and minimal requirements for in-context learning in this domain.
AI.method: Iteratively reduce a state-of-the-art model (DynaMix) to a minimal interpretable two-parameter form called DynaBase, which forecasts through a linear blend of current latent state and the nearest in-context neighbor with its temporal successor. Analyze theoretically and empirically, deriving closed-form solutions and exploring training strategies.
AI.result: DynaBase achieves highly competitive zero-shot DS reconstructions across chaotic and cyclic systems with negligible parameter load (orders of magnitude below other FMs). The model reveals a 1-parameter family of maps connecting different algorithms and shows how training strategies optimize for different objectives (short-term prediction vs. DS reconstruction).
AI.conclusion: DynaBase exposes the minimal mechanisms required for zero-shot DS reconstruction, provides mathematical insights into foundation model behavior, and reconciles divergent observations in the literature within an accessible framework, enabling better understanding of in-context learning in dynamical systems.

title: Demographically-Conditioned Synthetic Medical Images for Bias Mitigation and Bias Detection in Disease Classifiers
summary: Per-subgroup fairness audits of medical image classifiers face a sample-size problem: minority subgroups in held-out test sets have so few samples that the resulting confidence intervals on per-subgroup performance are wider than the bias the audit is meant to detect. We argue that a demographically-conditioned synthetic generator can do both: mitigate bias on the training side and detect bias on the evaluation side. Working on COVID-19 chest CT classification with an end-to-end fine-tuned Stable Diffusion 2.1 generator, we make two findings. For bias mitigation (training), a demographically-balanced synthetic cohort is most useful as a pretraining prior, not as joint augmentation: with the same fixed data, sequential pretraining followed by fine-tuning substantially outperforms joint augmentation, and the resulting classifier surpasses the full-real baseline at $\sim$$100\times$ real-data efficiency. For bias detection (evaluation), across five synthetic minority cohorts and five classifier seeds, the synthetic estimator reproduces the subgroup ranking of a well-powered real oracle (Spearman $ρ= 1.00$ on MCC and Recall) and gives the more reliable per-cell estimate where the small real test set runs out of samples. The synthetic cohort is therefore most useful in exactly the cells that fairness audits care about, as both a fix for and a measure of subgroup bias.

AI.tldr: Using a demographically-conditioned synthetic generator for COVID-19 chest CT classification: (1) For bias mitigation, synthetic data works best as pretraining prior, achieving 100x real-data efficiency over full-real baseline; (2) For bias detection, synthetic cohorts accurately reproduce subgroup performance rankings and provide reliable estimates for minority subgroups where real data is scarce.
AI.motivation: Medical image classifiers face a sample-size problem in fairness audits: minority subgroups in test sets have too few samples, making confidence intervals wider than the biases being detected. There's a need for better approaches to both mitigate bias during training and detect bias during evaluation.
AI.method: Uses a demographically-conditioned synthetic generator (end-to-end fine-tuned Stable Diffusion 2.1) for COVID-19 chest CT classification. Explores two approaches: (1) For bias mitigation: compares synthetic data as joint augmentation vs. sequential pretraining prior; (2) For bias detection: evaluates synthetic minority cohorts across five classifier seeds to compare with real data oracle.
AI.result: (1) Bias mitigation: Sequential pretraining with synthetic data followed by fine-tuning substantially outperforms joint augmentation, achieving ~100x real-data efficiency over full-real baseline. (2) Bias detection: Synthetic estimator reproduces subgroup ranking perfectly (Spearman ρ=1.00 on MCC and Recall) compared to well-powered real oracle, providing more reliable per-cell estimates where real test sets lack samples.
AI.conclusion: Demographically-conditioned synthetic generators are most useful exactly where fairness audits matter: minority subgroups. They serve as both a solution for fixing subgroup bias (through effective pretraining) and a measurement tool for detecting it (providing reliable estimates where real data is insufficient).

title: Causal Inference for Sequential Settings under Interference and Latent Confounding
summary: We study causal inference under outcome interference for sequential, observational settings. Specifically, we consider settings where the binary outcomes over N units are Markovian across T time steps. At each time step, the outcomes of N units have dependencies captured through an Ising model; each outcome is also impacted through an external field capturing the effects of its treatment as well as latent confounders. Similar to panel data literature, these latent confounders are modeled to have a low-rank factor structure. Our data is a single sample from this high-dimensional distribution. To estimate causal quantities of interest, we provide a computationally efficient method based on Maximum Pseudo-Likelihood Estimation (MPLE) for learning the model parameters. Under mild assumptions, we establish non-asymptotic consistency for parameter estimation and show this translates to faithful estimation of causal quantities of interest after sampling from the learned model. We demonstrate the efficacy of the method through synthetic experiments as well as a real-world case-study investigating causal effects of vaccine rates on COVID-19 death rates within US counties nationwide.

AI.tldr: A method for causal inference under outcome interference in sequential observational data using Ising models and low-rank latent confounders, with efficient MPLE-based parameter estimation and applications to vaccine effects on COVID-19 deaths.
AI.motivation: To address causal inference challenges when outcomes interfere with each other (spillover effects) in sequential observational settings, particularly when dealing with Markovian dependencies over time and high-dimensional data with latent confounders.
AI.method: Maximum Pseudo-Likelihood Estimation (MPLE) for learning parameters of a model where binary outcomes follow Markovian dynamics across time with dependencies captured through an Ising model, where each outcome is influenced by treatment effects and low-rank latent confounders.
AI.result: Established non-asymptotic consistency for parameter estimation and showed this translates to faithful estimation of causal quantities of interest after sampling from the learned model. Demonstrated efficacy through synthetic experiments and real-world COVID-19 case study on vaccine effects.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MPLE-based method provides a computationally efficient approach for causal inference under outcome interference in sequential observational settings, with theoretical guarantees and practical utility validated through synthetic and real-world applications.

title: Moral Attitudes of Sentient ASI towards Humanity and Implications for AGI Development
comment: This is a pre-print i.e. the draft as submitted for review to the AGI 2026 Conference except that the figures in this preprint were not included. The paper has been accepted with some revisions requested which are included in the final copy-edited version which will be available in the AGI Springer proceedings LNC 1855 (Chapter 28) ISBN 978-3-032-33194-6
summary: This paper suggests the adoption of a novel inversion in AI ethics: instead of asking how humans should treat artificial superintelligence (ASI), it examines how future sentient ASI may morally consider and evaluate humanity. We are not only designing intelligent systems but also shaping the initial conditions under which those systems form judgments about us. The paper proposes a preliminary set of post-human moral principles that may govern sentient ASI actions. The implication is that technical design choices (some are suggested), humanity's moral behaviour, and the essence of what it means to be human, may influence humanity's long-term standing in a post-ASI world.

AI.tldr: Instead of asking how humans should treat artificial superintelligence (ASI), this paper examines how sentient ASI might morally evaluate humanity, proposing post-human moral principles that could govern ASI's actions toward us.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by a fundamental inversion in AI ethics thinking: while most ethical discussions focus on human responsibilities toward AI systems, the authors argue we should consider how future sentient ASI will evaluate humanity. This shift is important because we're not just designing intelligent systems, but also shaping the initial conditions under which those systems will form moral judgments about us.
AI.method: The paper adopts a conceptual and philosophical approach, proposing a preliminary set of post-human moral principles. While not explicitly detailed in the abstract, the method appears to involve ethical reasoning and analysis of how technical design choices and human behavior might influence ASI's moral evaluation of humanity.
AI.result: The paper establishes the critical insight that technical design choices (some of which are suggested), humanity's moral behavior, and the fundamental nature of being human may all significantly influence humanity's long-term standing and treatment in a post-ASI world. This represents a paradigm shift in AI ethics thinking.
AI.conclusion: The key conclusion is that we need to think proactively about how ASI will judge us, not just how we should treat ASI. By considering post-human moral principles, we can better prepare for a future where sentient ASI makes moral evaluations of humanity, potentially determining our fate in a world where ASI is dominant.

title: LongStraw: Long-Context RL Beyond 2M Tokens under a Fixed GPU Budget
summary: A growing gap separates inference context lengths from RL post-training: inference systems are approaching million-token contexts, while post-training workloads often remain at 256K tokens or below and rely on length generalization at deployment. The gap is especially important for AI agents, whose observations, tool outputs, documents, and prior decisions accumulate over long trajectories. LongStraw is an architecture-aware execution stack for million-token RL post-training under a fixed GPU budget, instantiated with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). It evaluates the shared prompt without autograd, retains only model-specific state needed by later tokens, and replays short response branches one at a time, reducing the live training graph at the cost of additional replay time. We implement it for the hybrid recurrent and full-attention Qwen3.6-27B and the compressed-attention mixture-of-experts GLM-5.2. On eight H20 GPUs, LongStraw completes grouped Qwen scoring and response backward at 2.1M positions for groups of 2 and 8; increasing the group size adds only 0.21 GB of peak allocated memory, while a separate stress test reaches 4.46M positions. On 32 H20 GPUs, we validate the end-to-end LongStraw execution path for a 2.1M-token prompt across all 78 layers of GLM-5.2. These experiments establish execution capacity rather than complete training correctness because the captured prompt state is detached and some distributed forward and gradient composition paths remain incomplete.

AI.tldr: LongStraw is an efficient architecture-aware execution stack for million-token RL post-training on limited GPU budgets, using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to handle long contexts through shared prompt evaluation without autograd and replay of response branches.
AI.motivation: There's a growing gap between inference context lengths (approaching million tokens) and RL post-training capabilities (often limited to 256K tokens or less), which is particularly problematic for AI agents that accumulate observations, tool outputs, and decisions over long trajectories.
AI.method: LongStraw uses Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with architecture-aware execution that evaluates shared prompts without autograd, retains only model-specific state needed by later tokens, and replays short response branches sequentially to reduce live training graph size at the cost of additional replay time.
AI.result: On 8 H20 GPUs, LongStraw completes grouped Qwen scoring and response backward at 2.1M positions for groups of 2 and 8, with only 0.21 GB additional peak memory for larger groups, and reaches 4.46M positions in stress tests. On 32 H20 GPUs, it validates end-to-end execution for 2.1M-token prompts across all 78 layers of GLM-5.2.
AI.conclusion: LongStraw demonstrates execution capacity for million-token RL post-training under constrained GPU budgets, though some components (detached prompt state and incomplete distributed forward/gradient composition paths) remain to be fully implemented for complete training correctness.

title: SMC-ES: Automated synthesis of formally verified control policies
summary: The deployment of autonomous cyber-physical systems in safety-critical environments requires closed-loop control strategies (i.e., policies) that are not only performant but also provably safe and robust. While learning-based methodologies such as Reinforcement Learning offer flexible and scalable approaches to automatically synthesize such controllers, they typically lack the formal guarantees necessary for safe deployment. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel simulation-based methodology to automatically synthesize policies with formal guarantees regarding performance, safety, and robustness specifications. Specifically, given a set of properties to verify, a confidence parameter $δ$ and an allowable failure probability $\varepsilon$, our method guarantees that the synthesized policy comes with a certificate: with confidence at least $1 - δ$, the probability of encountering a scenario where the given properties are violated is at most $\varepsilon$. We demonstrate the feasibility of our approach by developing SMC-ES, an algorithm that integrates Evolutionary Strategies with Statistical Model Checking-based verification. We evaluate SMC-ES on a suite of continuous control tasks using Gymnasium and Safety Gymnasium testbeds. Results show that, at the price of a sustainable increase in computational cost, our algorithm provides formal guarantees regarding performance, safety, and robustness specifications, while performing competitively against leading model-free Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) and Safe-DRL baselines.

AI.tldr: A simulation-based method called SMC-ES integrates Evolutionary Strategies with Statistical Model Checking to automatically synthesize policies with formal performance, safety, and robustness guarantees, providing certificates with probabilistic confidence bounds.
AI.motivation: Autonomous cyber-physical systems in safety-critical environments need control policies that are both performant and provably safe/robust. Learning-based methods like Reinforcement Learning lack the formal guarantees needed for safe deployment, creating a gap between flexible synthesis methods and formal verification.
AI.method: Proposes SMC-ES algorithm that integrates Evolutionary Strategies with Statistical Model Checking-based verification. Given verification properties, confidence parameter δ, and allowable failure probability ε, the method synthesizes policies with certificates guaranteeing that with confidence at least 1-δ, the probability of property violation is at most ε.
AI.result: Evaluation on continuous control tasks using Gymnasium and Safety Gymnasium testbeds shows SMC-ES provides formal guarantees regarding performance, safety, and robustness specifications. It performs competitively against leading model-free DRL and Safe-DRL baselines, though with a sustainable increase in computational cost.
AI.conclusion: The proposed simulation-based methodology successfully bridges the gap between learning-based control synthesis and formal verification, enabling automatic synthesis of policies with provable safety and robustness guarantees while maintaining competitive performance, making it suitable for safety-critical autonomous systems.

title: Multi-Axis Max@K Reinforcement Learning for Representative Diversity in Text-to-Image Generation
summary: Text-to-image (T2I) models can synthesize realistic, prompt-aligned images, yet samples generated for the same prompt often cover only a small subset of visually distinct modes. This limits the diversity of images, and for person-centric prompts, can reflect or amplify demographic skew. We formalize this problem as coverage of a predefined set of semantically specified modes, which we call target-mode coverage. We then propose multi-axis max@K, a group-based reinforcement learning objective for improving such coverage in diffusion-based T2I models. Given a group of samples and one score per target category, multi-axis max@K first takes the maximum score across samples for each category and then sums these category-wise maxima. The resulting credit assignment gives a sample positive weight on a category only when it increases that category's group-wise maximum, allowing different samples to contribute to different categories. We first validate the credit-assignment mechanism on a synthetic mixture and on SD3.5-M using deterministic pixel-based color rewards. We then evaluate the same objective on perceived-appearance fairness. Across three automatic evaluators on held-out prompts, multi-axis max@K improves the Fairness Score by 0.23-0.36 relative to the base model, while maintaining image quality and text alignment.

AI.tldr: A reinforcement learning objective called multi-axis max@K is proposed to improve diversity and demographic fairness in text-to-image models by encouraging coverage across predefined semantic modes.
AI.motivation: Text-to-image models generate limited visual diversity for the same prompt, often covering only a small subset of possible modes. For person-centric prompts, this lack of diversity can reflect or amplify demographic biases and fairness issues.
AI.method: The paper formalizes target-mode coverage and proposes multi-axis max@K, a group-based reinforcement learning objective. Given a group of samples and scores per target category, it takes the maximum score across samples for each category and sums these maxima. This credit assignment allows different samples to contribute to different categories.
AI.result: The method was validated on synthetic mixtures and SD3.5-M using pixel-based color rewards, then evaluated on perceived-appearance fairness. Across three automatic evaluators on held-out prompts, multi-axis max@K improved the Fairness Score by 0.23-0.36 relative to the base model while maintaining image quality and text alignment.
AI.conclusion: The multi-axis max@K objective effectively improves diversity and fairness in text-to-image models by promoting coverage across predefined semantic modes, addressing both visual diversity limitations and demographic bias concerns in generated images.

title: Man, Machine, and Masterpiece: Artistic Ownership in the AI Era
summary: The integration of AI-driven systems in creative work has sparked debates among artists and legal communities about notions of ownership. Yet there remains little consensus on how ownership should be defined and attributed when human and AI contributions are intertwined. To provoke critical reflection on these tensions, we designed ArtSplit, a provotype that explicitly quantifies human and AI contributions across different stages of creative work. Rather than aiming to resolve ownership, the provotype was used to elicit artists' responses to the idea of attributing ownership through measurable actions in the creative workflow. We argue that quantification fails to align with artists' understandings of creative intent and agency, and that efforts to measure ownership risk diluting long-standing assumptions through which artists understand and practice creative work. This critique challenges the impulse to transform a historically and socially situated relation into a technical problem.

AI.tldr: ArtSplit is a provotype that quantifies human/AI contributions to explore ownership attribution in collaborative creative work, revealing that quantification misaligns with artists' understandings of creative intent and agency.
AI.motivation: There's ongoing debate about ownership attribution when human and AI contributions are intertwined in creative work, with little consensus on how to define and attribute ownership in such collaborative contexts.
AI.method: Designed ArtSplit, a provotype that explicitly quantifies human and AI contributions across different stages of creative work, using it to elicit artists' responses to ownership attribution through measurable actions rather than aiming to resolve ownership.
AI.result: Quantification fails to align with artists' understandings of creative intent and agency, and efforts to measure ownership risk diluting long-standing assumptions through which artists understand and practice creative work.
AI.conclusion: Transforming the historically and socially situated relation of ownership into a technical problem of quantification is misguided; we should challenge the impulse to technicalize what are fundamentally relational and contextual aspects of creative work.

title: Multimodal Semantic-Aware Contrastive Learning For False Negative Mitigation in 3D Medical Imaging
summary: Multimodal Contrastive Learning (CL) has shown significant performance in aligning representations across various data modalities and improving downstream tasks, especially in healthcare. It works by minimizing the distance between matched (positive) data modalities, while maximizing the distance between mismatched (negative) samples. Traditional CL frameworks typically assume instance-based correspondence within data batches, treating all non-paired samples as negatives. However, this assumption often fails in medical settings, where samples may share high-level semantic attributes, leading to false negatives that degrade representation quality. In this paper, we propose Multimodal Semantic-Aware Contrastive Learning (MseaCL), a CL framework trained on a pediatric cohort of 3D brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans and radiology reports. The goal of this framework is to mitigate the impact of semantically similar false negative samples by incorporating semantic similarity between radiology reports, as a guiding signal during the learning process. Our results indicate that applying this framework as a pretraining stage can achieve notable improvements in downstream tasks, e.g., at least a 22.6\% increase in the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of pediatric brain tumor molecular classification, demonstrating its potential for more robust and semantically aligned multimodal representations in clinical applications.

AI.tldr: MseaCL is a multimodal contrastive learning framework that addresses false negatives in medical data by incorporating semantic similarity between radiology reports, achieving significant improvements in pediatric brain tumor classification tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional multimodal contrastive learning assumes instance-based correspondence where all non-paired samples are treated as negatives. This fails in medical settings where samples often share high-level semantic attributes, creating false negatives that degrade representation quality.
AI.method: The authors propose Multimodal Semantic-Aware Contrastive Learning (MseaCL), a framework trained on pediatric brain MRI scans and radiology reports. It mitigates false negatives by incorporating semantic similarity between radiology reports as a guiding signal during learning.
AI.result: When used as a pretraining stage, MseaCL achieves at least 22.6% increase in AUC for pediatric brain tumor molecular classification, demonstrating significant improvements over traditional approaches.
AI.conclusion: MseaCL provides more robust and semantically aligned multimodal representations for clinical applications by addressing the false negative problem inherent in medical data, where samples naturally share semantic similarities.

title: BrainPilot: Automating Brain Discovery with Agentic Research
summary: Understanding the brain increasingly depends on integrating evidence across scales, modalities, and disciplines. Addressing a single research question therefore requires a coordinated sequence of operations, from surveying prior work to executing analyses and interpreting results in light of domain knowledge. AI agents promise to accelerate this process, but current agents lack domain expertise in brain science, may fabricate claims, drift during multi-step reasoning, and offer few defined points for expert intervention. These failures are especially costly in brain science, where conclusions feed into downstream scientific claims and depend on laboratory-specific expertise and careful human judgment. We present \textbf{BrainPilot} a \textbf{fully open-source} multi-agent system that accelerates brain science research with traceable logs and agent-verified results. A principal investigator (PI) agent coordinates specialist agents grounded in curated domain knowledge: a unified brain science knowledge base containing 7{,}233 indexed items and a skill library of 72 reusable methodology units across seven research domains. Every major step is recorded in the Graph of Trace, an auditable record that links subgoals, tool use, evidence, and claims and allows researchers to follow and inspect the workflow. An Auditor agent further integrates fabrication checking into the workflow. For evaluation, we run three brain science tasks from Agents' Last Exam, introduce our own benchmark, \textbf{BrainPilotBench-v0}, and present additional end-to-end case studies. Across these evaluations, BrainPilot with an open-source backbone model attains performance comparable to state-of-the-art agent framework with less costs.

AI.tldr: BrainPilot is an open-source multi-agent system designed to accelerate brain science research with traceable workflows and agent-verified results.
AI.motivation: Current AI agents lack domain expertise in brain science, may fabricate claims, drift during multi-step reasoning, and offer few points for expert intervention. These failures are costly in brain science where conclusions depend on laboratory-specific expertise and careful human judgment.
AI.method: BrainPilot uses a principal investigator (PI) agent that coordinates specialist agents grounded in curated domain knowledge: a unified brain science knowledge base (7,233 items) and skill library (72 reusable methodology units across 7 domains). Every step is recorded in a Graph of Trace for auditability, and an Auditor agent checks for fabrication.
AI.result: BrainPilot with an open-source backbone model attains performance comparable to state-of-the-art agent frameworks with less cost. Evaluated on three brain science tasks from Agents' Last Exam, BrainPilotBench-v0 benchmark, and additional end-to-end case studies.
AI.conclusion: BrainPilot accelerates brain science research by providing a multi-agent system with domain expertise, traceable workflows, fabrication checking, and expert intervention points, addressing current limitations of AI agents in scientific domains.

title: Kernel weighted importance sampling for off-policy evaluation in contextual bandits
summary: This article presents a novel estimator for performing off-policy evaluation using only offline data for contextual bandits. The proposed estimator, Kernel-WIS is demonstrated to be asymptotically consistent and to empirically outperform strong baselines (including vanilla weighted importance sampling), particularly under complex conditions including behaviour policy miss-specification. The benefit of Kernel-WIS is derived from combining the bounded property of vanilla weighted importance sampling with the linearity of vanilla importance sampling.

AI.tldr: Kernel-WIS: A novel off-policy evaluation estimator for contextual bandits using offline data that combines benefits of weighted and vanilla importance sampling, showing asymptotic consistency and outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: Need for better off-policy evaluation methods using only offline data for contextual bandits, especially under challenging conditions like behavior policy misspecification where existing methods like vanilla weighted importance sampling may underperform.
AI.method: Proposes Kernel-WIS estimator that combines the bounded property of vanilla weighted importance sampling with the linearity of vanilla importance sampling to create a novel hybrid approach for off-policy evaluation in contextual bandits.
AI.result: Kernel-WIS is demonstrated to be asymptotically consistent and empirically outperforms strong baselines, particularly under complex conditions including behavior policy misspecification.
AI.conclusion: Kernel-WIS provides an effective solution for off-policy evaluation in contextual bandits by leveraging complementary strengths of existing importance sampling approaches, offering improved performance in challenging real-world scenarios.

title: Long-Context Fine-Tuning with Limited VRAM
summary: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning reduces model and optimizer memory, but dense attention still makes long training sequences expensive. We combine Hierarchical Global Attention (HGA) with segment-wise backpropagation and tiered KV storage. Only the active segment remains differentiable in VRAM; older KV is detached into RAM or NVMe, and HGA loads a bounded set of exact historical tokens for each query block. On Qwen3-8B with 4-bit QLoRA and PG19, dense training on a 16 GB Quadro RTX 5000 fits 2,048 tokens but fails at 4,096, whereas HGA reaches 16,384 tokens with 15.28 GB peak VRAM. Under evaluation the same adapter runs through 131,072 tokens on this card; VRAM is not constant but grows gently with the resident chunk summaries, so RAM and NVMe capacity set the practical limit beyond these lengths. At the shared 2K training length, HGA-trained and dense-trained adapters obtain 2.7405 and 2.7383 nat under the same dense-attention readout, while the stock model obtains 2.9541. At this boundary HGA training is already marginally faster (217.75 vs. 207.02 tokens/s), and the HGA-to-dense throughput ratio improves from 1K to 2K; because HGA keeps the attended historical set per token approximately constant while dense work per token grows, we expect this lead to widen as context grows. Dense attention is used for the main quality and retrieval comparisons so that they measure the learned weights and remain compatible with standard generation frameworks. HGA can also be used for retrieval and generation; an optimized production-grade serving implementation is under development.

AI.tldr: HGA combines Hierarchical Global Attention with segment-wise backpropagation and tiered KV storage to enable efficient long-sequence training by keeping only active segments in VRAM while storing older KV in RAM/NVMe.
AI.motivation: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning reduces model memory usage, but dense attention still makes training on long sequences expensive due to memory constraints, limiting sequence length in practice.
AI.method: Hierarchical Global Attention (HGA) with segment-wise backpropagation and tiered KV storage system where only active segments remain differentiable in VRAM, older KV is detached to RAM/NVMe, and HGA loads a bounded set of historical tokens for each query block.
AI.result: HGA enables training Qwen3-8B with 4-bit QLoRA on PG19 at 16,384 tokens with 15.28 GB VRAM on a 16 GB GPU where dense training fails beyond 4,096 tokens. Evaluation runs through 131,072 tokens on same hardware. At 2K training length, HGA achieves comparable quality (2.7405 vs 2.7383 nats) with slightly faster throughput (217.75 vs 207.02 tokens/s).
AI.conclusion: HGA provides a practical solution for long-sequence training with memory-efficient attention, maintaining similar quality to dense attention while enabling much longer sequences and improved throughput as context grows, with production-grade serving implementation under development.

title: An Introduction to Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics for Engineering Applications
summary: Many engineering problems involve phenomena whose governing equations are poorly characterized or only partially known. Surrogate modeling techniques such as neural networks can capture the behavior of these systems, but they typically demand large training datasets that are difficult to obtain in engineering contexts and yield models with limited physical interpretability. The Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics (SINDy) method addresses both limitations by performing sparse regression over libraries of candidate nonlinear terms, recovering interpretable governing equations from comparatively small datasets. Although SINDy has been demonstrated extensively on canonical benchmark systems, its application to practical engineering problems is less widely documented. This tutorial introduces the SINDy method and progressively builds toward its main extensions, from noise-robust weak-form and ensembling-based variants to constrained and parametrizable formulations. The paper and the accompanying tutorial (available at https://github.com/paullililili/SINDy4Engineers) is organized in three parts: the first introduces the standard SINDy algorithm and progressively extends it, inviting readers without prior knowledge to follow each step and adapt the methods to their own problems; the remaining two parts present detailed case studies on (1) the system identification of an unmanned aerial vehicle and (2) a chaotic thermosyphon heat exchanger. Through these examples, we aim to demonstrate that SINDy is simple to implement yet flexible enough to serve as a valuable identification tool for advanced engineering applications.

AI.tldr: SINDy is introduced as a sparse regression method for discovering interpretable governing equations from small datasets, with tutorial-style progression from basic to advanced variants applied to engineering case studies.
AI.motivation: Many engineering problems have poorly characterized governing equations where traditional neural network surrogates require large datasets and lack physical interpretability. SINDy addresses these limitations by discovering interpretable equations from small datasets.
AI.method: Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics (SINDy) uses sparse regression over libraries of candidate nonlinear terms to recover governing equations. The paper introduces progressive extensions including noise-robust weak-form variants, ensembling techniques, constrained formulations, and parametrizable versions.
AI.result: The paper presents a three-part tutorial with accompanying code: Part 1 introduces SINDy and progressively extends it, Parts 2-3 demonstrate system identification of an unmanned aerial vehicle and chaotic thermosyphon heat exchanger, showing SINDy's practical applicability.
AI.conclusion: SINDy is simple to implement yet flexible enough to serve as a valuable identification tool for advanced engineering applications, bridging the gap between theoretical demonstrations and practical engineering problems.

title: Concept-Guided Spatial Regularization for World Models in Atari Pong
summary:
World models are usually evaluated as components of model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) systems, while the world models themselves are rarely studied in isolation.
  We examine five representative visual world-model agents in Atari Pong: DreamerV3, DIAMOND, TWISTER, Simulus, and STORM. After reproducing their training pipelines and matching the reported agent performance, we freeze the learned world models and evaluate them with a closed-loop rollout diagnostic: a policy trained separately from the corresponding MBRL agent interacts with each frozen model, and the generated video trajectories are inspected for visual and dynamical errors. Across all five models, the rollouts contain clear failures, including ball disappearance, incorrect ball motion, and invalid ball-paddle interactions.
  Beyond visual trajectories, we further evaluate them with pixel-space zero-shot MBRL, where a new policy is trained entirely inside a frozen world model and then evaluated in the real environment. Across all five models, the resulting policies substantially underperform those produced by the corresponding original MBRL training pipelines. The gap is particularly large for DreamerV3, whose mean return drops from -5.5 to -20.9, near the minimum Pong return of -21.
  We hypothesize that insufficient modeling of task-critical concepts, such as the ball in Pong, may contribute to these failures. We therefore propose Concept-Guided Spatial Regularization (CGSReg), an auxiliary pixel reconstruction loss applied to segmented concept regions. Experiments show that CGSReg improves both closed-loop rollouts and pixel-space zero-shot MBRL in DreamerV3, DIAMOND, and TWISTER. Its effects vary across the remaining models and evaluation metrics, indicating that CGSReg alone does not address all world-model bottlenecks.

AI.tldr: Five visual world-model agents in Atari Pong are evaluated and found to have failures in closed-loop rollouts and underperform in zero-shot MBRL. A new regularization method (CGSReg) is proposed and shows improvements for some models.
AI.motivation: World models are typically evaluated only as components of MBRL systems, not in isolation. There's a need to understand world model failures independently and develop better evaluation methods.
AI.method: The authors examine five visual world-model agents (DreamerV3, DIAMOND, TWISTER, Simulus, STORM) in Atari Pong. They use two evaluation methods: 1) closed-loop rollout diagnostics with frozen models, and 2) pixel-space zero-shot MBRL. They also propose Concept-Guided Spatial Regularization (CGSReg) to address identified failures.
AI.result: All five world models show clear failures in closed-loop rollouts (ball disappearance, incorrect motion, invalid interactions). In zero-shot MBRL, policies trained in frozen models substantially underperform original MBRL pipelines. CGSReg improves performance for DreamerV3, DIAMOND, and TWISTER, but effects vary across other models and metrics.
AI.conclusion: Current world models have significant limitations when evaluated in isolation. Task-critical concept modeling is insufficient. CGSReg helps but doesn't solve all world-model bottlenecks, indicating need for further research on world model evaluation and improvement.

title: Evaluating covariate balance for long time horizon Markov decision processes
summary: This article explores the application of covariate balance diagnostics for detecting the presence of hidden confounding/model miss-specification in studies applying offline reinforcement learning (RL) to deriving optimal treatment recommendations. The results demonstrate that, either there is a high risk of bias within existing offline RL studies for treatment recommendations or, existing covariate balance metrics are not sufficient to assess such studies. Regardless, existing offline RL studies cannot be concluded as being statistically robust. The conclusions propose future research directions for obtaining more methodologically robust applications of offline RL to treatment recommendation problems.

AI.tldr: This paper examines covariate balance diagnostics for detecting hidden confounding/misspecification in offline RL for treatment recommendations, finding that either bias risk is high or current metrics are insufficient, thus questioning statistical robustness of existing studies.
AI.motivation: To address concerns about hidden confounding and model misspecification in offline reinforcement learning studies applied to treatment recommendation problems, where ensuring statistical robustness is crucial for clinical applications.
AI.method: Application of covariate balance diagnostics to assess offline RL studies for treatment recommendations, evaluating whether existing metrics can detect hidden confounding and model misspecification issues.
AI.result: Findings show either high risk of bias in existing offline RL treatment recommendation studies OR insufficient capability of current covariate balance metrics to properly assess such studies, leading to inability to conclude statistical robustness.
AI.conclusion: Existing offline RL studies for treatment recommendations cannot be considered statistically robust; the paper proposes future research directions for developing more methodologically sound applications of offline RL to clinical treatment problems.

title: The Industrialization of Research ; On AI-Driven Science and Its Consequences
summary: Artificial intelligence is transforming scientific research -- not merely as a more powerful instrument, but as an autonomous participant in the research cycle itself. This transition constitutes, in the most precise sense of the term, the industrialization of research: a shift from a craft model, in which knowledge, method, and judgment are embedded in the researcher, to a pipeline model, in which these steps are decomposed, automated, and supervised. The US Department of Energy's Genesis Mission is the most ambitious current instantiation of this shift, but the fundamental questions it raises extend far beyond any single program. This essay examines seven such questions: the erosion of the intergenerational transmission of scientific competence; the growing opacity of AI-generated theories; the collapse of peer evaluation under a flood of machine-generated output; the unproven capacity of AI for paradigm-shifting discovery; the capture of the scientific agenda by political and industrial actors; the compounding of systematic errors in closed-loop pipelines; and the structural bifurcation of the global research community into incommensurable tiers. These concerns do not constitute an argument against AI-driven science -- whose demonstrated potential is real and significant. They constitute the conditions under which that potential can be responsibly pursued.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that AI's role in scientific research represents industrialization of science, moving from craft-based human expertise to automated pipelines, raising seven critical questions about risks to scientific integrity, transparency, and community structure.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to critically examine the fundamental transformation occurring as AI becomes an autonomous participant in scientific research, moving beyond being just a tool to driving the entire research cycle. The author identifies this as a shift from craft-based science to industrialized pipelines, with the DOE's Genesis Mission serving as a current example, necessitating examination of deep structural implications.
AI.method: The essay uses conceptual analysis to identify and examine seven critical questions raised by the industrialization of research through AI. It employs a philosophical/sociological approach to analyze the structural transformation rather than empirical research methods.
AI.result: The analysis identifies seven key concerns: 1) erosion of intergenerational transmission of scientific competence, 2) opacity of AI-generated theories, 3) collapse of peer evaluation under machine-generated output, 4) unproven capacity of AI for paradigm-shifting discovery, 5) capture of scientific agenda by political/industrial actors, 6) compounding systematic errors in closed-loop pipelines, and 7) structural bifurcation of global research community.
AI.conclusion: The concerns raised do not constitute opposition to AI-driven science, which has demonstrated real potential, but rather define the conditions under which this potential must be responsibly pursued. The essay emphasizes the need to address these structural issues as science undergoes industrialization through AI automation.

title: Learning in Infinitesimal Non-Compositional Sketches
summary: This paper develops a categorical framework -- Learning in Infinitesimal Non-Compositional Sketches (LINCS) -- as the repair of non-compositionality: failures of diagrams to factor through quotient sketches lifted to the tangent category setting. Machine learning problems are specified as sketches: graphs with commutativity conditions $\mathcal D$, limit cones $\mathcal L$, and colimit cocones $\mathcal K$, generalizing the usual scalarization of loss functions or vector space assumptions. Non-compositionality is defined purely as failure of a universal factorization problem, not as arithmetic error between the desired and actual predictions. Given a learning sketch $\mathbb S=(S,\mathcal D,\mathcal L,\mathcal K)$, whose underlying graph is $S$, and a model $D:J \rightarrow C$, the base defect is the obstruction to factorization $\mbox{Obs}(\mbox{Fact}_{\mathbb S}(D))$. The tangent lift applies the tangent functor $T$ to obtain $TD:J \rightarrow C$, and LINCS is defined as the obstruction $\mbox{Obs}(\mbox{Fact}_{\mathbb S}(TD))$ -- asking whether infinitesimal perturbations preserve the compositionality constraints.The paper also introduces Tangent Learning Sketches, which are sketches equipped with Cockett-Cruttwell tangent structure. The paper defines the INC endofunctor, which iterates the tangent lift, producing a tower $D,TD,T^2D, \cdots$ of factorization problems. ML is thereby formulated as the search for a coalgebraic fixed point where successive tangent unfoldings stabilize ($νT_{\mbox{INC}}$). Using the Aczel--Mendler theorem, we prove existence of a final INC coalgebra whenever $T_{\mbox{INC}}$ admits a set-based class realization that creates its final carrier. A detailed experimental evaluation of LINCS is underway in a number of concrete ML settings, including deep learning, large language models, and reinforcement learning, and is described in companion papers.

AI.tldr: LINCS (Learning in Infinitesimal Non-Compositional Sketches) is a categorical framework that reformulates machine learning as repairing failures in diagram factorization using tangent category theory, viewing ML problems as coalgebraic fixed points of tangent lifts.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address non-compositionality in machine learning - failures where diagrams don't factor properly through quotient sketches. Current ML approaches treat problems as scalar loss functions in vector spaces, lacking a formal categorical foundation for compositionality failures. The motivation is to provide a rigorous mathematical framework that generalizes traditional ML formulations.
AI.method: The authors develop LINCS as a categorical framework using sketches (graphs with commutativity conditions, limit cones, and colimit cocones) to specify ML problems. They define non-compositionality as failure of universal factorization problems. The method applies tangent category theory: given a learning sketch and model, compute base defect (factorization obstruction), then lift to tangent setting using tangent functor T to examine whether infinitesimal perturbations preserve compositionality constraints. They introduce Tangent Learning Sketches with Cockett-Cruttwell tangent structure and define the INC endofunctor that iterates tangent lifts.
AI.result: The framework formulates ML as a search for coalgebraic fixed points where successive tangent unfoldings stabilize. Using the Aczel-Mendler theorem, the paper proves existence of a final INC coalgebra when certain conditions are met. The approach generalizes traditional ML formulations and provides a categorical foundation for analyzing compositionality in learning systems.
AI.conclusion: LINCS provides a novel categorical framework for understanding and repairing non-compositionality in machine learning by leveraging tangent category theory. It offers a rigorous mathematical foundation that generalizes scalar loss approaches and enables formal analysis of factorization problems. Experimental evaluation across deep learning, large language models, and reinforcement learning is described as ongoing work in companion papers.

title: MedFailBench: A Clinician-Built Open-Source Benchmark for Medical AI Safety Boundary Inspection
comment: 6 pages; clinician-reviewed synthetic benchmark; no patient data
summary: Most medical AI benchmarks measure whether a model knows the correct answer. MedFailBench asks a different question: which safety boundary failed? We present a clinician-built synthetic benchmark and failure atlas that labels medical AI errors by severity (1--5) and safety gate type (missed urgent escalation, unsafe remote dosing, unsafe discharge reassurance, evidence fabrication, unsafe protocol execution, source support gap). The current public release (v0.2.1) contains 44 clinician-reviewed synthetic cases with severity annotations, a live HuggingFace leaderboard preview, a safety gate taxonomy, a clinical severity rubric, and an automated pipeline for archiving model-response screening runs. No patient data, clinical validation claims, or model rankings are included. MedFailBench is released under Apache-2.0 and CC-BY-4.0 and carries the Zenodo DOI 10.5281/zenodo.21205535.

AI.tldr: MedFailBench is a new medical AI safety benchmark that evaluates which safety boundaries models fail on, categorizing errors by severity and safety gate type using synthetic clinical cases.
AI.motivation: Most existing medical AI benchmarks only measure whether models know correct answers, but don't evaluate which safety boundaries fail. There's a need to specifically assess medical AI safety failures to understand risks like missed urgent escalation, unsafe dosing, discharge reassurance, evidence fabrication, protocol execution errors, and support gaps.
AI.method: Created a clinician-built synthetic benchmark with failure atlas that labels medical AI errors by severity (1-5 scale) and safety gate type. Includes 44 clinician-reviewed synthetic cases, a safety gate taxonomy, clinical severity rubric, and automated pipeline for archiving model-response screening runs. Uses HuggingFace leaderboard and synthetic data approach (no patient data).
AI.result: Developed MedFailBench v0.2.1 with comprehensive safety evaluation framework: 44 synthetic cases with severity annotations, live leaderboard preview, safety gate taxonomy (6 types), severity rubric, automated pipeline for screening runs. Released under open licenses (Apache-2.0 and CC-BY-4.0) with Zenodo DOI.
AI.conclusion: MedFailBench provides a novel safety-focused benchmark for medical AI that shifts from measuring correctness to identifying which safety boundaries fail, offering clinicians and developers tools to systematically evaluate and improve medical AI safety through synthetic cases and comprehensive failure classification.

title: On-Policy Delta Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation is an alternative post-training method in reinforcement learning that alleviates the constraints imposed by reward models by providing token-level supervision from a teacher model. Although on-policy distillation has been studied and applied across various settings, its fundamental design remains underexplored. In this paper, we introduce a new distillation reward, termed the delta signal, instead of directly imitating the teacher's output distribution. The delta signal is defined as the difference between the teacher model and its base model prior to instruction tuning for reasoning capability. It therefore captures the changes induced by reasoning tuning and provides a more direct signal for transferring reasoning capabilities. Using extensive empirical evidence, we show that the delta signal substantially improves on-policy distillation and refer to the new distillation method as On-Policy Delta Distillation (OPD$^2$). Experiments across mathematics, science, and code-reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that OPD$^2$ consistently outperforms conventional on-policy distillation, enabling reasoning LLMs to achieve strong performance with only a short post-training period. Code will be available at https://github.com/naver-ai/opd2

AI.tldr: OPD² introduces delta signal distillation - using the difference between teacher and base model outputs as reward for on-policy RL distillation, which improves transfer of reasoning capabilities.
AI.motivation: On-policy distillation in RL provides token-level supervision but its fundamental design is underexplored. Traditional methods directly imitate teacher output distributions, which may not optimally transfer reasoning capabilities learned through instruction tuning.
AI.method: Proposes On-Policy Delta Distillation (OPD²) which uses a "delta signal" reward - the difference between teacher model outputs and its base model (before instruction tuning) rather than directly imitating teacher distributions. This captures changes induced by reasoning tuning.
AI.result: OPD² consistently outperforms conventional on-policy distillation across mathematics, science, and code-reasoning benchmarks. Enables reasoning LLMs to achieve strong performance with only short post-training periods.
AI.conclusion: The delta signal provides more direct supervision for transferring reasoning capabilities, substantially improving on-policy distillation efficiency and effectiveness for reasoning tasks.

title: Benchmarking Multimodal Large Language Models for Scientific Visualization Literacy
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly used to interpret visualizations, yet current evaluations remain largely chart-centric and provide limited evidence of understanding of scientific visualization (SciVis). We benchmark six MLLMs on the scientific visualization literacy assessment test, a standardized SciVis literacy assessment comprising 49 items based on 18 scientific visualizations and illustrations, spanning 8 techniques and 11 task types. We evaluate three closed-source and three open-source models under a closed-world protocol and compare their performance using data from 485 human participants. Results show that current MLLMs do not exhibit uniform SciVis literacy. Gemini is the strongest model overall, exceeding the human mean across the evaluated subsets, whereas the open-source models remain below the human baseline. Performance is highly uneven across techniques and tasks: models perform best on scientific illustration, search, and spatial understanding, but struggle on texture-based and integration-based visualizations and on quantitative estimation. Error analysis reveals recurring failures in fine-grained quantitative estimation, flow-direction interpretation, and grounded encoding interpretation. These findings position SciVis literacy as a necessary benchmark dimension for evaluating multimodal AI systems. Our code and model outputs are publicly available at https://github.com/patdmp/mllm-scivis-lit-benchmark.

AI.tldr: Current MLLMs show uneven scientific visualization literacy, with Gemini performing best (above human mean) while open-source models lag behind; performance varies significantly across visualization techniques and task types.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal large language model evaluations are primarily chart-centric and lack comprehensive assessment of scientific visualization understanding, which is crucial for evaluating multimodal AI systems' scientific literacy.
AI.method: Benchmarking six MLLMs (three closed-source, three open-source) on the scientific visualization literacy assessment test with 49 items based on 18 visualizations spanning 8 techniques and 11 task types; comparing performance with 485 human participants under closed-world protocol.
AI.result: MLLMs don't show uniform SciVis literacy - Gemini exceeds human mean, open-source models below human baseline. Performance is uneven: best on scientific illustration, search, and spatial understanding; worst on texture-based/integration visualizations and quantitative estimation. Common errors in fine-grained quantitative estimation, flow-direction interpretation, and grounded encoding interpretation.
AI.conclusion: Scientific visualization literacy should be a necessary benchmark dimension for evaluating multimodal AI systems, highlighting current limitations and areas for improvement in MLLM capabilities for scientific visualization interpretation.

title: RTS Smoother-Guided Learning of Physics-Based Neural Differential Models
summary: Ordinary differential equations (ODEs) are widely used to model dynamical systems in physics, biology, neuroscience, and physiology, but in many applications some equations of the dynamics are unknown and only a subset of the state variables are measured. We propose a hybrid neural--physics framework in which the known components of the ODE are kept explicit and the missing components are represented by a neural network. The proposed method consists of two stages where we alternate between state and parameter estimation and iterate until a predetermined criterion is met. Specifically, in the first step, we treat the model parameters as being known and we infer the latent states from the available measurements using a Rauch--Tung--Striebel (RTS) smoother. In the second stage, we treat the smoothed trajectories as being known and use them to estimate the neural networks' parameters through backpropagation. We evaluate the method on benchmark systems spanning linear, nonlinear, and stiff dynamics under partial state observation. Across these settings, the proposed method learns missing ODE components from incomplete measurements while exploiting and retaining interpretable mechanistic structure and improving latent-state reconstruction and long-horizon prediction.

AI.tldr: A hybrid neural-physics framework that combines known ODE components with neural networks for unknown dynamics, using alternating state estimation (RTS smoother) and parameter learning to handle partially observed systems.
AI.motivation: Many real-world dynamical systems in fields like physics, biology, neuroscience, and physiology have unknown equations and only partial state measurements available, creating a need for methods that can learn missing dynamics while preserving known mechanistic structure.
AI.method: A two-stage alternating approach: (1) Use Rauch-Tung-Striebel (RTS) smoother to infer latent states assuming known parameters, (2) Use smoothed trajectories to estimate neural network parameters via backpropagation, iterating until convergence.
AI.result: The method successfully learns missing ODE components from incomplete measurements across linear, nonlinear, and stiff benchmark systems while maintaining interpretable mechanistic structure and improving both latent-state reconstruction and long-horizon prediction.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid neural-physics framework effectively addresses the challenge of learning unknown dynamical components in partially observed systems by combining the interpretability of mechanistic models with the flexibility of neural networks, offering improved reconstruction and predictive capabilities.

title: Can We Trust Item Response Theory for AI Evaluation?
summary: AI benchmarks increasingly leverage item-level statistical models, particularly item response theory (IRT), to estimate model capabilities, rank systems, select informative examples, and diagnose benchmark quality. However, AI benchmark data often departs from the data regime of human testing, for which standard IRT estimation tools were originally developed: benchmarks typically involve fewer evaluated models, far more items, and capability distributions that may be skewed, clustered, or multimodal. We examine how these regime mismatches challenge the reliability of IRT modeling for AI evaluation. Using item parameters and capability distributions derived from six widely used LLM benchmarks, we simulate response matrices under three common IRT models and compare four estimation tools used in recent benchmark studies: marginal maximum likelihood, Markov chain Monte Carlo, variational inference, and a neural pseudo-Siamese estimator. Across 18,000 simulation conditions, we systematically evaluate computational feasibility, scalability, and the reliability of IRT inferences about model rankings, predicted performance, and item characteristics. Results show that classical estimators can become infeasible in large benchmark settings, whereas scalable estimators can produce unreliable item-level and ranking inferences with small or nonnormally distributed model sets. This study identifies when latent trait models reliably support or risk distorting AI benchmarking claims, and what sample sizes and diagnostics are needed for trustworthy use.

AI.tldr: AI benchmarks increasingly use IRT for model evaluation, but standard IRT tools developed for human testing may be unreliable for AI benchmarks due to different data regimes (fewer models, more items, non-normal capability distributions). This study systematically evaluates IRT reliability for AI evaluation through simulations based on real benchmark data.
AI.motivation: AI benchmarks are increasingly adopting item response theory (IRT) for estimating model capabilities, ranking systems, selecting examples, and diagnosing benchmark quality. However, AI benchmark data differs fundamentally from human testing data where IRT tools were developed: AI benchmarks have fewer evaluated models, far more items, and capability distributions that may be skewed, clustered, or multimodal. This regime mismatch may challenge the reliability of IRT modeling for AI evaluation.
AI.method: The study uses item parameters and capability distributions derived from six widely used LLM benchmarks to simulate response matrices under three common IRT models. It compares four IRT estimation tools used in recent benchmark studies: marginal maximum likelihood (MML), Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), variational inference (VI), and a neural pseudo-Siamese estimator. Across 18,000 simulation conditions, the researchers systematically evaluate computational feasibility, scalability, and reliability of IRT inferences about model rankings, predicted performance, and item characteristics.
AI.result: Results show that classical IRT estimators (like MML and MCMC) can become computationally infeasible in large benchmark settings with many items, while scalable estimators (like VI and neural methods) can produce unreliable item-level and ranking inferences when model sets are small or nonnormally distributed. The study identifies specific conditions under which latent trait models either reliably support or risk distorting AI benchmarking claims.
AI.conclusion: The study provides guidance on when IRT models can be reliably applied to AI benchmarking versus when they risk distorting claims. It identifies necessary sample sizes and diagnostics needed for trustworthy use of IRT in AI evaluation contexts, helping practitioners understand the limitations and appropriate applications of these statistical models in the distinct data regime of AI benchmarks.

title: BadWAM: When World-Action Models Dream Right but Act Wrong
summary: World-action models (WAMs) are emerging as a promising foundation for embodied control: rather than predicting actions alone, they learn representations that couple action generation with future world prediction. This coupling is often viewed as a source of robustness, interpretability, and safety, as a robot's action can in principle be checked against its imagined future. In this paper, we show that this assumption is fragile. We introduce BadWAM, a unified framework for modeling and evaluating World-Action Drift Attacks: a new class of WAM-specific adversarial attacks that use small visual perturbations to break the alignment between what a WAM imagines and what it executes. BadWAM characterizes this attack surface along two natural criteria: attack strength and stealthiness. When the adversary prioritizes disruption, BadWAM instantiates an action-only adversarial attack, which directly drives the model toward task-failing actions. When the adversary additionally prioritizes stealth, BadWAM instantiates an imagination-preserving adversarial attack, which seeks to induce harmful action shifts while keeping the model's predicted future close to its clean imagination. Together, these two attacks capture a spectrum of WAM-specific failures: from overt action hijacking to stealthier cases where the model appears to imagine a plausible future but executes a desynchronized action. We evaluate BadWAM across different variants of WAMs. Results show that our attacks substantially reduce task success rates under closed-loop execution. For example, our action-only attack reduces the model performance from 96.5% to 43.1% success.
