The results of our imagination-preserving attack further exposes a WAM-specific vulnerability: moderate future-preserving regularization can maintain strong attack performance while reducing future imagination drift.

AI.tldr: BadWAM introduces World-Action Drift Attacks on World-action models (WAMs), showing these models are vulnerable to visual perturbations that break alignment between imagined futures and executed actions through action-only and imagination-preserving attacks.
AI.motivation: World-action models (WAMs) are emerging as promising for embodied control by coupling action generation with future world prediction, which is assumed to provide robustness, interpretability, and safety. However, the authors question this assumption, arguing it's fragile and vulnerable to specific attacks.
AI.method: The authors introduce BadWAM, a unified framework for modeling and evaluating World-Action Drift Attacks. These are WAM-specific adversarial attacks using small visual perturbations to break alignment. BadWAM characterizes attacks along two criteria: attack strength and stealthiness. It instantiates two types: (1) action-only adversarial attacks that directly drive models toward task-failing actions, and (2) imagination-preserving adversarial attacks that induce harmful action shifts while keeping predicted futures close to clean imagination.
AI.result: The attacks substantially reduce task success rates under closed-loop execution. The action-only attack reduces model performance from 96.5% to 43.1% success. The imagination-preserving attack exposes a WAM-specific vulnerability: moderate future-preserving regularization can maintain strong attack performance while reducing future imagination drift.
AI.conclusion: The assumption that coupling action generation with future world prediction in WAMs inherently provides robustness and safety is fragile. World-Action Drift Attacks demonstrate that WAMs are vulnerable to adversarial perturbations that can break the alignment between imagined futures and executed actions, compromising their supposed safety benefits.

title: Plover: Steering GUI Agents through Plan-Centric Interaction
summary: Graphical user interface (GUI) automation remains challenging in real-world environments, where dynamic layouts, unexpected dialogs, and evolving interface states can cause autonomous agents to drift from user intent. Recent vision-based multimodal agents improve flexibility by operating directly over screenshots and natural language instructions, but planning and adaptation often remain internal, limiting users' ability to inspect, supervise, or correct system behavior. We present Plover, a plan-centric vision-based GUI automation system that externalizes task plans and replanning as persistent, inspectable, and revisable artifacts. Through a planner--executor architecture, Plover supports explicit supervision of evolving execution, localized correction through editable plans, natural-language guidance, and screenshot-grounded interventions, while preserving prior progress during repair. A formative study with six participants informed the interaction design. We then evaluate Plover through benchmark failure-case repair and scenario-based workflow analyses. Our results show that many autonomous GUI-agent failures are structurally repairable when plans remain visible and interventions are localized, and that explicit replanning helps make GUI automation more transparent, controllable, and adaptable.

AI.tldr: Plover is a vision-based GUI automation system that makes task plans external, inspectable, and revisable to improve transparency, control, and adaptability when agents encounter failures.
AI.motivation: GUI automation faces challenges in real-world environments with dynamic layouts, unexpected dialogs, and evolving interface states that can cause agents to drift from user intent. Current vision-based agents lack transparency and user control as planning remains internal.
AI.method: Plover uses a planner-executor architecture that externalizes task plans and replanning as persistent artifacts. It supports explicit supervision of execution, localized correction through editable plans, natural-language guidance, and screenshot-grounded interventions while preserving progress during repair.
AI.result: Benchmark failure-case repair and workflow analyses show that many autonomous GUI-agent failures are structurally repairable when plans remain visible and interventions are localized. Explicit replanning makes GUI automation more transparent, controllable, and adaptable.
AI.conclusion: Externalizing plans and replanning as inspectable artifacts enables more transparent, controllable, and adaptable GUI automation that better maintains user intent in dynamic environments.

title: Data Driven Block Replacement Scheduling
summary: We develop data-driven algorithms for maintaining $N$ independent identical machines under a \textit{block replacement policy}, in which each machine is replaced upon failure and all machines are jointly replaced at regular intervals of length $k$. The goal is to learn the cost-minimizing interval $k^*$ from operational data when the lifetime distribution is unknown. At each decision epoch, the operator selects $k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$, observes the resulting failure history (a mixture of complete and right-censored lifetimes) and incurs a per-unit-time cost governed by the renewal function. We formulate this as a stochastic multi-armed bandit and propose Hoeffding- and Bernstein-based lower-confidence-bound algorithms achieving $O(K \log T)$ regret, matching the Lai--Robbins lower bound. Exploiting a nested observation property unique to block replacement, correlated variants attain $O((K-k^*)\log T)$ regret and require only $O(1)$ direct pulls of suboptimal arms $k < k^*$. A complementary Kaplan--Meier renewal algorithm estimates the lifetime distribution nonparametrically from censored data, achieving almost-sure policy consistency and empirically near-zero incremental regret at long horizons. We additionally analyze two average-cost MDPs: a time-elapsed formulation establishing that block replacement is optimal within its policy class for any lifetime distribution, and an age-vector formulation proving a monotone threshold structure under increasing failure rate distributions and providing a gold-standard cost benchmark. Numerical experiments confirm the theoretical ordering and reveal structural cost gaps between optimal block and age-dependent replacement.

AI.tldr: Data-driven algorithms for learning cost-optimal block replacement intervals under unknown lifetime distributions using bandit approaches with logarithmic regret.
AI.motivation: Traditional block replacement policies require known lifetime distributions, but in practice these distributions are unknown. Need data-driven methods to learn optimal replacement intervals from operational data with censored failure observations.
AI.method: Formulate as stochastic multi-armed bandit problem; propose Hoeffding- and Bernstein-based lower-confidence-bound algorithms. Exploit nested observation property for correlated variants. Develop Kaplan-Meier renewal algorithm for nonparametric estimation. Analyze MDP formulations (time-elapsed and age-vector) to establish optimality properties.
AI.result: Algorithms achieve O(K log T) regret matching Lai-Robbins lower bound; correlated variants achieve O((K-k*) log T) regret with only O(1) direct pulls of suboptimal arms. Kaplan-Meier algorithm achieves almost-sure policy consistency. MDP analysis proves block replacement optimality within policy class and monotone threshold structure under increasing failure rates.
AI.conclusion: Data-driven approaches can effectively learn optimal block replacement intervals with strong theoretical guarantees. Block replacement proves optimal within its policy class, and the methods bridge theory with practical implementation using censored operational data.

title: Mutable Low-Rank Sketches for Retrain-Free Recommendation
summary: A common bottleneck in two-stage recommendation is embedding staleness: when a user rates a new item, their embedding remains fixed until the next retrain cycle. We propose mutable sketches, which store each user's preferences in a KP-tree (a sparse segment tree with sum aggregation), fit a low-rank projection once, and recompute embeddings on-the-fly as ratings arrive. We prove that each new observation monotonically tightens the prediction error envelope (Theorem 1), a guarantee that FunkSVD and eALS lack. On KuaiRec, the mutable sketch achieves 0.810 RMSE at 1.8% data read vs. ALS 0.822 at 100%, with 8x faster per-batch updates. A new user receives personalized recommendations in <1 ms after their first rating, with no model retraining required. A comparison of sampling strategies across density regimes shows that the KP-tree's norm-proportional sampling provides 40-130% better item coverage on sparse data (<1% density), while uniform sampling suffices on dense matrices.

AI.tldr: Mutable sketches solve embedding staleness in two-stage recommendation by using KP-trees for dynamic preference storage and on-the-fly embedding recomputation, achieving better performance with faster updates and immediate personalization.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the problem of embedding staleness in two-stage recommendation systems, where user embeddings remain fixed until the next retrain cycle even when users rate new items, limiting the system's responsiveness to new interactions.
AI.method: Proposes mutable sketches that store user preferences in KP-trees (sparse segment trees with sum aggregation), fit a low-rank projection once, and recompute embeddings on-the-fly as ratings arrive. Uses norm-proportional sampling for better item coverage in sparse data.
AI.result: Achieves 0.810 RMSE at 1.8% data read vs ALS 0.822 at 100% on KuaiRec, with 8x faster per-batch updates. New users get personalized recommendations in <1ms after first rating. Provides 40-130% better item coverage on sparse data (<1% density) with norm-proportional sampling.
AI.conclusion: Mutable sketches offer a practical solution to embedding staleness with theoretical guarantees (monotonic error envelope tightening), significantly reducing computational requirements while maintaining or improving recommendation quality, enabling real-time personalization without retraining.

title: When Words Are Safe But Actions Kill: Probing Physical Danger Beyond Text Safety in Hidden-State Risk Space
summary: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly serve as high-level planners for embodied agents, where linguistically benign instructions can become unsafe once grounded in the physical world. We study whether this physically grounded danger is the same safety problem as ordinary text-level content danger. Through hidden-state direction analysis and random-split null tests, we show that content danger (CD) and physical danger (PD) form separable signals in LLM representations across Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B/32B, Phi-3.5 and SmolLM2. Building on the CD/PD separability, we propose PRISM, a single-layer L2-regularized logistic probe over full hidden states. PRISM achieves 86.2--87.7\% accuracy on SafeAgentBench with 11.7--13.7\% FPR, while same-scale LLM judges over-block safe tasks at 24.7--39.0\% FPR. We further introduce PhysicalSafetyBench-1K (PSB-1K), a contrastive benchmark of 1{,}000 physical-risk pairs without direct harm keywords, to test whether methods detect physically grounded danger rather than explicit unsafe wording. On PSB-1K, PRISM reaches 99.6\% accuracy and 0.7\% FPR, whereas a Qwen2.5-3B judge rejects 67.8\% of safe tasks. PRISM also replicates on SafeText and EARBench, supporting hidden-state probing as a representation-level method for physical safety beyond text moderation.

AI.tldr: PRISM is a safety probing method that separates content danger from physical danger in LLM representations for safer embodied agents, achieving high accuracy on physical safety benchmarks.
AI.motivation: When LLMs serve as planners for embodied agents, linguistically benign instructions can become unsafe once grounded in the physical world. The paper questions whether physically grounded danger is the same safety problem as ordinary text-level content danger.
AI.method: The paper shows through hidden-state direction analysis and random-split null tests that content danger (CD) and physical danger (PD) form separable signals in LLM representations. Building on this separability, they propose PRISM - a single-layer L2-regularized logistic probe over full hidden states.
AI.result: PRISM achieves 86.2-87.7% accuracy on SafeAgentBench with 11.7-13.7% FPR, significantly outperforming same-scale LLM judges which over-block safe tasks at 24.7-39.0% FPR. On their new PhysicalSafetyBench-1K, PRISM reaches 99.6% accuracy and 0.7% FPR, showing it detects physical danger rather than just unsafe wording.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that content danger and physical danger are separable in LLM representations, and PRISM provides an effective representation-level method for physical safety that goes beyond text moderation. This supports hidden-state probing as a valuable approach for safer embodied agents.

title: Decoding Market Emotion from Blockchain Activity: A Data-Driven Sentiment Classifier
comment: This manuscript has been accepted for presentation at the IEEE International Symposium on Computers and Communications (ISCC 2026)
summary: The growing use of Bitcoin as a decentralized digital asset and investment tool has sparked strong interest in understanding its market behavior. This study presents a new approach to analyze Bitcoin market sentiment by combining on-chain and financial data with social media posts. Unlike models that aim to predict prices, this work focuses on explaining market sentiment using blockchain transactions, historical price data of Bitcoin, and daily Twitter sentiment classifications. The method merges sentiment trends with on-chain and financial metrics, normalized into a dataset for detailed market analysis. Multiple machine learning models were tested using cross-validation, with Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) emerging as the most reliable model for classifying sentiment, achieving an average F1-score of about 0.84. SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), a game theory-based method for model interpretability, was used to quantify the contribution of on-chain features to the model's predictions, improving transparency. The results indicate that this data combination yields meaningful predictive signals and insights, supporting data-driven cryptocurrency analysis and future improvements with deep learning.

AI.tldr: A new framework combines on-chain, financial, and social media data to explain Bitcoin market sentiment, using Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) with SHAP for interpretability, achieving strong predictive performance.
AI.motivation: Growing Bitcoin usage as decentralized asset creates need for better market understanding; existing models focus on price prediction rather than sentiment explanation.
AI.method: Combine on-chain data (blockchain transactions), financial data (historical prices), and social media sentiment (Twitter classifications) into normalized dataset; test multiple ML models with cross-validation; use Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) as primary model with SHAP for feature importance analysis.
AI.result: XGBoost achieved average F1-score of ~0.84 for sentiment classification; combination of data sources provides meaningful predictive signals; SHAP analysis quantified contributions of on-chain features to predictions.
AI.conclusion: Integrated data approach yields valuable insights for cryptocurrency analysis, supports data-driven decision-making, and provides foundation for future deep learning enhancements.

title: AutoSynthesis: An agentic system for automated meta-analysis
summary: Evidence synthesis is crucial for turning primary research into reliable knowledge for science, medicine, education, and policy. Yet, quantitative evidence synthesis remains largely manual and difficult to scale. Here, we introduce AutoSynthesis, an end-to-end multi-agent system for automated meta-analysis. Given a research question in natural language, AutoSynthesis formulates a search strategy, retrieves scientific literature, screens candidate studies, assesses full-text eligibility, extracts quantitative statistics, computes standardized effect sizes, and finally performs random-effects meta-analysis. AutoSynthesis further supports heterogeneity analysis to examine how effect sizes vary across moderators, as well as risk-of-bias assessment. As output, AutoSynthesis produces a transparent report aligned with PRISMA guidelines. In our application, AutoSynthesis screened over 28 studies and extracted more than 20 quantitative claims. The pooled effect estimates produced by AutoSynthesis are similar to Hedges' $g$ of expert-conducted meta-analyses, indicating close agreement with manual evidence synthesis. Together, these results show that AutoSynthesis can make quantitative evidence synthesis more scalable, thereby supporting evidence-based decision-making across disciplines.

AI.tldr: AutoSynthesis is an end-to-end multi-agent system that automates meta-analysis from research question to final report, achieving results similar to expert-conducted meta-analyses.
AI.motivation: Quantitative evidence synthesis is crucial for science, medicine, education, and policy but remains largely manual and difficult to scale, creating a need for automation to support evidence-based decision-making.
AI.method: AutoSynthesis is a multi-agent system that takes natural language research questions and performs: search strategy formulation, literature retrieval, study screening, full-text eligibility assessment, quantitative statistics extraction, standardized effect size computation, and random-effects meta-analysis. It also supports heterogeneity analysis and risk-of-bias assessment.
AI.result: The system screened over 28 studies, extracted more than 20 quantitative claims, and produced pooled effect estimates similar to Hedges' g values from expert-conducted meta-analyses, showing close agreement with manual synthesis.
AI.conclusion: AutoSynthesis can make quantitative evidence synthesis more scalable and reliable, supporting evidence-based decision-making across various disciplines by automating the meta-analysis process while maintaining quality comparable to expert manual synthesis.

title: teLLMe Why (Ain't Nothing but a Jam): Exploratory Causal Analysis of Urban Driving Data
comment: Accepted at the NeurIPS 2025 Workshop on UrbanAI. 6 pages
summary: Traffic agencies now have access to large volumes of video-derived data for studying safety and congestion. Most of these data are observational and collected without interventions, which makes causal questions such as "How would rain change traffic density?" difficult to answer. We present teLLMe, a system for exploratory causal analysis of urban driving datasets. The system starts from a structured event table built from dashcam annotations and combines causal structure learning with the PC algorithm, bootstrap-based stability checks, and query-specific effect estimation using linear regression and DoWhy. Natural-language questions are mapped to structured causal queries through a schema-aware LLM, enabling users to specify treatments, outcomes, and subpopulations. teLLMe returns a "Causal Card" that summarizes effect estimates, adjustment sets, DAG support, and assumptions, followed by a short natural-language explanation. Case studies on BDD-derived traffic events show that the system can surface plausible relationships involving weather, peak hours, and traffic density, while making uncertainty and modeling choices explicit. The system is designed as a tool for hypothesis generation and expert reasoning rather than a source of definitive causal claims.

AI.tldr: teLLMe is a system for exploratory causal analysis of urban driving data that uses causal structure learning and LLM-powered natural language queries to generate causal insights from observational traffic data.
AI.motivation: Traffic agencies have large volumes of observational video data but struggle with causal questions because the data lacks interventions. Traditional methods can't answer questions like "How would rain change traffic density?"
AI.method: Combines causal structure learning with PC algorithm, bootstrap-based stability checks, and DoWhy for effect estimation. Uses schema-aware LLM to map natural-language questions to structured causal queries, then generates "Causal Cards" with effect estimates, adjustment sets, DAG support, and assumptions.
AI.result: Case studies on BDD-derived traffic events show the system can surface plausible relationships involving weather, peak hours, and traffic density while making uncertainty and modeling choices explicit.
AI.conclusion: teLLMe is designed as a tool for hypothesis generation and expert reasoning rather than a source of definitive causal claims, providing a practical approach for exploratory causal analysis of observational traffic data.

title: SearchOS-V1: Towards Robust Open-Domain Information-Seeking Agent Collaboration
summary: Recent advances in Tool-Integrated Large Language Models have made web search a core capability of information-seeking agents. However, as interaction histories grow, agents increasingly struggle to track task progress. When search attempts fail to yield useful evidence, current single- and multi-agent systems can become trapped in repetitive loops, wasting search budgets and ultimately compromising the quality and completeness of the final output. We introduce SearchOS, a system-level multi-agent framework that turns fragile, implicit search progress into explicit, persistent, and shared state. First, we formulate open-domain information seeking as relational schema completion with grounded citations, where agents discover entities, populate attributes across linked tables, and anchor each value to source evidence. Then we design Search-Oriented Context Management (SOCM), which externalizes the evolving state into Frontier Task, an Evidence Graph, a Coverage Map, and Failure Memory. Built on SOCM, SearchOS applies a pipeline-parallel scheduling mechanism that overlaps the execution of sub-agents and continuously refills freed slots with tasks targeting unresolved coverage gaps to improve utilization and throughput. To schedule and control the execution of search agents, SearchOS introduces a Search Tool Middleware Harness that intercepts model and tool interactions to record grounded evidence and react to stalls or budget exhaustion, and provides a reusable hierarchical skill system comprising strategy and access skills to augment the agents' search process and avoid repeating failed search patterns across runs. On WideSearch and GISA, SearchOS leads all metrics among the evaluated single- and multi-agent baselines, paving the way toward robust information-seeking collaboration.

AI.tldr: SearchOS is a multi-agent framework that improves web search performance by externalizing search progress into persistent shared state and using parallel scheduling to avoid repetitive loops.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based search agents struggle with tracking task progress as interaction histories grow, often getting trapped in repetitive search loops when attempts fail, which wastes search budgets and compromises output quality.
AI.method: SearchOS formulates information seeking as relational schema completion with citations, implements Search-Oriented Context Management (SOCM) with Frontier Task, Evidence Graph, Coverage Map, and Failure Memory, uses pipeline-parallel scheduling, and includes a Search Tool Middleware Harness with hierarchical skills.
AI.result: On WideSearch and GISA benchmarks, SearchOS outperformed all evaluated single- and multi-agent baselines across all metrics, demonstrating superior performance.
AI.conclusion: SearchOS provides a robust multi-agent framework for information-seeking collaboration by making search progress explicit and persistent, enabling efficient search orchestration and avoiding repetitive failure patterns.

title: Pretraining Data Can Be Poisoned through Computational Propaganda
summary: Poisoning pretraining data can introduce harmful behaviors to LMs that are difficult to detect and mitigate. Prior work on poisoning pretraining data has largely exploited established data sources such as Wikipedia, which do not represent the large scale and heterogeneity typical of pretraining corpora, and has ignored the interaction between poisoned data and data curation pipelines. We demonstrate that poisoning attacks on pretraining data are feasible beyond this limited setting through an existing web-scale content injection mechanism: public discussion interfaces. Additionally, to measure whether malicious content is included after web crawling and data curation, we introduce HalfLife, a novel analysis for estimating adversarial content inclusion in web-crawl based LM training data. We use HalfLife to explore the feasibility of poisoning pretraining corpora at web scale through open discussion interfaces. Our analysis demonstrates the importance of estimating whether poison injections are included in pretraining data, and establishes third-party webpage content as a possible vector for attacking language model pretraining.

AI.tldr: Poisoning attacks on pretraining data are feasible through public discussion interfaces, and a new method called HalfLife helps estimate adversarial content inclusion in web-crawl based LM training data.
AI.motivation: Prior work on poisoning pretraining data has been limited to established data sources like Wikipedia, which don't represent the large scale and heterogeneity of real pretraining corpora. Additionally, existing research has ignored the interaction between poisoned data and data curation pipelines. The paper aims to demonstrate that poisoning attacks are feasible in more realistic web-scale settings through public discussion interfaces.
AI.method: The authors introduce HalfLife, a novel analysis method for estimating adversarial content inclusion in web-crawl based language model training data. They use HalfLife to explore the feasibility of poisoning pretraining corpora at web scale through open discussion interfaces, specifically measuring whether malicious content is included after web crawling and data curation processes.
AI.result: The analysis demonstrates that poisoning attacks on pretraining data are indeed feasible beyond the limited settings previously studied. The research shows that third-party webpage content can serve as a viable vector for attacking language model pretraining, highlighting vulnerabilities in current data collection pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Poisoning pretraining data through public discussion interfaces presents a serious threat to language model safety. The study emphasizes the importance of estimating whether poison injections are actually included in pretraining data after curation processes, and establishes that third-party webpage content represents a significant attack vector that needs to be addressed in LM security.
